parts.content: Explain why aluminium is a better electrical conductor than sodium.
parts.markscheme:
- State that sodium has 1 valence electron and aluminium has 3 valence electrons <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that aluminium contributes more delocalized electrons to the sea of electrons, increasing conductivity <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify valence electrons
steps.content:
To understand conductivity in the metallic model, we first look at the number of valence electrons each atom contributes to the metal lattice. 

* **Sodium ($Na$):** Located in Group 1, it has 1 valence electron.
* **Aluminium ($Al$):** Located in Group 13, it has 3 valence electrons.

This fundamental difference is the starting point for explaining their physical properties, as covered in **Topic S3.1: The periodic table**.

steps.title: Apply metallic bonding model
steps.content:
In a metallic bond, valence electrons are not bound to a single atom; instead, they are 'delocalized' and form a 'sea of electrons' surrounding the positive metal ions. 

Because each aluminium atom contributes **three** electrons to this sea, while each sodium atom only contributes **one**, aluminium has a much higher density of delocalized electrons within its structure. This concept is explored in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**.

steps.title: Relate delocalization to conductivity
steps.content:
Electrical conductivity is the movement of these delocalized electrons when a potential difference (voltage) is applied. 

Since aluminium has a greater number of delocalized electrons per unit volume, there are more charge carriers available to move through the lattice. This makes aluminium a more efficient conductor of electricity than sodium.

highlights.text: Electrical and thermal conductivity depend on the mobility of electrons in metals.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To secure full marks on this question, you must include both points:
1.  **State the valence counts:** Sodium has 1 valence electron and aluminium has 3 valence electrons. <marks>A1</marks>
2.  **Explain the result:** Aluminium contributes more delocalized electrons to the 'sea,' which increases its electrical conductivity. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Recall the metallic model
steps.content:
To understand electrical conductivity in metals, we look at the **metallic bonding model**. Metals consist of a lattice of positive metal ions (cations) surrounded by a "sea" of **delocalized electrons** that are free to move throughout the structure and carry an electric charge.

This is covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**.

highlights.text: mobility of electrons in metals

steps.title: Identify valence electrons
steps.content:
We first identify the number of valence electrons for each element based on its group in the Periodic Table:

*   **Sodium ($\mathrm{Na}$)**: Group 1, so it has **1** valence electron.
*   **Magnesium ($\mathrm{Mg}$)**: Group 2, so it has **2** valence electrons.
*   **Aluminium ($\mathrm{Al}$)**: Group 13, so it has **3** valence electrons.

In a metallic lattice, these valence electrons become the delocalized electrons.

steps.title: Analyze charge carrier density
steps.content:
As we move from $\mathrm{Na}$ to $\mathrm{Mg}$ to $\mathrm{Al}$ across Period 3, each atom contributes more electrons to the delocalized "sea":

*   $\mathrm{Na}$ contributes $1e^-$ per atom.
*   $\mathrm{Mg}$ contributes $2e^-$ per atom.
*   $\mathrm{Al}$ contributes $3e^-$ per atom.

Because there are more delocalized electrons available to move and carry current within the same volume, the **density of mobile charge carriers** increases.

steps.title: State and justify order
steps.content:
Since electrical conductivity is proportional to the density of these mobile electrons, the conductivity increases in the following order:

$$\mathrm{Na} < \mathrm{Mg} < \mathrm{Al}$$

**Markscheme Breakdown:**
*   **1 mark**: Stating the correct order ($\mathrm{Na} < \mathrm{Mg} < \mathrm{Al}$).
*   **1 mark**: Explaining that the number of delocalized electrons increases from 1 to 2 to 3.
*   **1 mark**: Linking this to a higher density of mobile charge carriers.

steps.title: Identify the elements
steps.content:
The question asks about the physical trends for the alkali metals: lithium ($Li$), sodium ($Na$), and potassium ($K$). These are all found in **Group 1** of the periodic table. 

You can review these patterns in **Topic S3.1: The periodic table**, specifically looking at group trends.

highlights.text: Lithium, sodium, and potassium are Group 1 metals.

steps.title: Recall the group trend
steps.content:
For Group 1 metals, the melting point follows a very specific pattern as you move from the top of the group to the bottom. 

From your study of periodicity, you should recall that as the atomic number increases down this specific group, the melting point **decreases**.

steps.title: State the final trend
steps.content:
Based on the periodic patterns of the alkali metals, the trend is that the melting point decreases down the group.

This happens because the metallic bond becomes weaker as the ionic radius increases, making it easier to overcome the attractive forces and melt the metal.

steps.title: Identify Group 1 elements
steps.content: The question asks for the trend in melting point for **Lithium (Li)**, **Sodium (Na)**, and **Potassium (K)**, which are the first three elements in **Group 1** of the periodic table.

highlights.text: Lithium, sodium, and potassium are Group 1 metals.

steps.title: Locate data in booklet
steps.content: To find the specific melting points, we refer to the **IB Chemistry Data Booklet**, specifically **Table 6: Physical properties of the elements**. This table lists the melting points ($T_m$) for each element in Kelvin (K).

steps.title: Compare melting point values
steps.content:
According to Table 6, the melting points for these elements are:

*   **Lithium (Li):** $454\text{ K}$
*   **Sodium (Na):** $371\text{ K}$
*   **Potassium (K):** $337\text{ K}$

As we move down the group from Li to Na to K, the numerical value of the melting point clearly becomes smaller.

steps.title: State the trend
steps.content: By observing the values from the data booklet, we can conclude that the **melting point decreases** as you go down Group 1.

steps.title: Identify the Group 1 elements
steps.content: The question asks about the trend in melting points for Group 1 metals: lithium ($Li$), sodium ($Na$), and potassium ($K$). To understand this trend, we need to look at how the strength of their metallic bonds changes as we move down the group.

highlights.text: Lithium, sodium, and potassium are Group 1 metals.

steps.title: Analyze atomic radius change
steps.content:
As you move down Group 1, each successive element has an additional occupied electron shell. This means that the **atomic radius increases** as we go from $Li$ to $Na$ to $K$.

This concept is a core part of **Topic 3: Periodicity**, specifically regarding group trends in electron configurations.

steps.title: Relate radius to bond strength
steps.content:
Metallic bonding is defined as the electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions and a "sea" of delocalized electrons. 

As the atomic radius increases, the distance between the positive nucleus of the metal ion and the delocalized valence electrons increases. According to Coulomb's Law, as distance increases, the force of attraction **weakens**. 

You can review the nature of these attractions in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**.

steps.title: State the melting point trend
steps.content:
Since the attraction between the nuclei and the delocalized electrons becomes weaker down the group, less thermal energy is required to overcome these metallic bonds and melt the metal.

Therefore, the **melting point decreases** down Group 1.

steps.title: Identify group membership
steps.content: The question specifies that lithium, sodium, and potassium are all **Group 1 metals**. In chemistry, elements in the same group share similar electronic configurations and chemical properties. This is a fundamental concept covered in **Topic S3.1: The periodic table**.

highlights.text: Lithium, sodium, and potassium are Group 1 metals.

steps.title: Determine valence electrons
steps.content:
For main-group elements (Groups 1, 2, and 13–18), the group number tells us how many electrons are in the outermost shell (valence electrons). 

Since lithium ($Li$), sodium ($Na$), and potassium ($K$) are in **Group 1**, they each have exactly **one valence electron**.

steps.title: Define metallic delocalization
steps.content:
According to the metallic bonding model (found in **Topic S2.3.1**), a metal consists of a lattice of positive ions (cations) surrounded by a "sea" of **delocalized electrons**. 

These delocalized electrons come directly from the valence shells of the metal atoms. Because each atom in these three metals has only one valence electron to offer, they each contribute one electron to the delocalized pool.

steps.title: Compare the number of electrons
steps.content:
Because lithium, sodium, and potassium all belong to the same group and have the same number of valence electrons, the number of delocalized electrons is the same for all three. 

Each metal contributes **1 delocalized electron per atom**.

steps.title: Identify group and configurations
steps.content:
To determine the number of delocalized electrons, we first look at the electronic configurations of these elements. Since they are all in Group 1, they have similar valence shell configurations.

Using the rules from **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**, we can write their condensed configurations:

- Lithium (Li): $[He] 2s^1$
- Sodium (Na): $[Ne] 3s^1$
- Potassium (K): $[Ar] 4s^1$

highlights.text: Lithium, sodium, and potassium are Group 1 metals.

steps.title: Compare valence electrons
steps.content:
By comparing these specific valence shell configurations ($2s^1$, $3s^1$, and $4s^1$), we can see that each atom contains exactly **one** electron in its outermost shell.

In metallic bonding (covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**), these valence electrons are lost from the individual atoms to form a "sea" of delocalized electrons surrounding the positive metal ions.

steps.title: State the comparison
steps.content:
Because each atom of Lithium, Sodium, and Potassium has only one valence electron to offer, they all contribute the **same number** of delocalized electrons per atom.

$$\text{Number of delocalized electrons per atom} = 1$$

steps.title: Identify periodic group
steps.content: To understand the number of delocalized electrons, we first look at the position of lithium, sodium, and potassium in the periodic table. These elements are all in **Group 1**.

highlights.text: Lithium, sodium, and potassium are Group 1 metals.

steps.title: Determine cation charge
steps.content:
In a metallic lattice, metal atoms lose their outer (valence) electrons to form a "sea" of delocalized electrons surrounding positive metal cations ($M^{+}$). 

Since lithium, sodium, and potassium are all in Group 1, they each have one valence electron. When they form a metallic lattice, each atom loses that single electron to become a cation with a charge of $1+$.

steps.title: Compare delocalized electrons
steps.content:
The number of delocalized electrons contributed to the lattice per atom is equal to the charge of the cation formed. Because all three metals form a $1+$ cation ($Li^{+}$, $Na^{+}$, and $K^{+}$), they all contribute the same number of electrons.

Therefore, each metal contributes **one delocalized electron per atom**.

highlights.text: All contribute 1 delocalized electron per atom

steps.title: Identify the Group trend
steps.content: In Group 1, as we move from lithium ($Li$) to potassium ($K$), the melting point decreases. To explain this using the metallic bonding model, we need to look at the strength of the attraction between the metal cations and the "sea" of delocalized electrons.

highlights.text: Lithium, sodium, and potassium are Group 1 metals.

steps.title: Radius and charge density
steps.content:
As you move down Group 1, the number of occupied electron shells increases, which causes the **atomic and ionic radius to increase**. 

Because each Group 1 metal forms a $1+$ ion, the positive charge remains the same, but it is spread over a larger volume. This results in a **lower charge density** for the larger cations.

steps.title: Evaluate bond strength
steps.content:
Metallic bonding is the electrostatic attraction between the positive metal ions and the delocalized valence electrons. 

With a larger ionic radius and lower charge density, the attraction between the nucleus of the cation and the delocalized electrons becomes **weaker**. The electrons are, on average, further away from the positive nuclei.

steps.title: Relate to melting point
steps.content: Since the metallic bonds are weaker in larger atoms like potassium compared to smaller atoms like lithium, **less thermal energy** is required to overcome these attractions and break the lattice structure. Therefore, the melting point decreases down the group.

steps.title: Analyze electron configurations
steps.content: To compare the number of delocalized electrons, we first need to look at the electron configurations of potassium ($Z=19$) and iron ($Z=26$). The number of delocalized electrons in a metal typically corresponds to the electrons in the outer shells that are free to move through the lattice.

steps.title: Evaluate Potassium (K)
steps.content:
Potassium is a Group 1 alkali metal. Its electron configuration is:

$$[Ar] 4s^1$$

Because it has only one electron in its valence shell, each potassium atom contributes **1 delocalized electron** to the metallic bond.

highlights.text: $\mathrm{K}$ contributes 1 delocalized electron

steps.title: Evaluate Iron (Fe)
steps.content:
Iron is a transition metal. Its electron configuration is:

$$[Ar] 3d^6 4s^2$$

In transition metals, the energy levels of the $4s$ and $3d$ subshells are very close. This allows electrons from both the $4s$ and the partially filled $3d$ orbitals to become delocalized. As a result, iron contributes **more delocalized electrons** per atom (typically 2 or more) compared to potassium.

highlights.text: $\mathrm{Fe}$ contributes more (variable, typically 2 or more) due to partially filled $d$-orbitals

steps.title: Final Comparison
steps.content:
In summary:
- **Potassium (K):** 1 delocalized electron per atom.
- **Iron (Fe):** More than 1 (variable) delocalized electrons per atom.

This difference occurs because transition metals like iron can utilize their $d$-subshell electrons for metallic bonding, whereas main-group metals like potassium are limited to their $s$-block valence electrons.

steps.title: Analyze Potassium (Group 1)
steps.content:
First, let's look at Potassium ($K$). It is an alkali metal located in **Group 1** of the periodic table. 

Because it is in Group 1, it has only one valence electron (electron configuration: $[Ar] 4s^1$). In a metallic lattice, each potassium atom contributes this **one** valence electron to the sea of delocalized electrons.

This is a fundamental property of metallic bonding covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**.

highlights.text: K contributes 1 delocalized electron

steps.title: Analyze Iron (Transition Metal)
steps.content:
Next, we look at Iron ($Fe$). Unlike potassium, iron is a **transition metal** found in the d-block. 

Transition metals have a more complex electron configuration ($[Ar] 3d^6 4s^2$). Because the energy levels of the $4s$ and $3d$ orbitals are very close, electrons from both subshells can be delocalized. This means iron contributes **more** electrons to the sea of delocalized electrons per atom (typically 2 or more).

highlights.text: Fe contributes more (variable, typically 2 or more) due to partially filled d-orbitals

steps.title: Compare the contributions
steps.content:
To answer the comparison directly:

*   **Potassium ($K$):** Contributes 1 delocalized electron per atom.
*   **Iron ($Fe$):** Contributes more delocalized electrons per atom than potassium.

This difference occurs because transition metals like iron involve their **d-block electrons** in metallic bonding, whereas Group 1 metals like potassium only involve their single outer **s-orbital electron**.

steps.title: Define metallic bonding factors
steps.content:
To understand why iron has a higher melting point, we first need to look at what holds a metal together. Metallic bonding is the **electrostatic attraction** between a lattice of positive metal ions (cations) and a "sea" of delocalized electrons.

According to **Topic S2.3.2: Strength of metallic bonds**, the strength of this bond depends on two main factors:
1. The **charge** of the metal ion.
2. The **ionic radius** of the metal ion.

steps.title: Compare ionic charges
steps.content:
First, let's compare the charges of the ions. 

Potassium ($K$) is in Group 1, so it forms $K^{+}$ ions with a $+1$ charge. Iron ($Fe$) is a transition metal and typically forms $Fe^{2+}$ or $Fe^{3+}$ ions. 

Because the iron ions have a **higher charge** than the potassium ions, they provide more delocalized electrons to the "sea" and exert a stronger pull on them.

highlights.text: Fe^{2+} or Fe^{3+} has a higher charge than K^+

steps.title: Analyze charge density
steps.content:
Next, we consider the size of the ions. Iron atoms are smaller than potassium atoms, and consequently, the **iron ions have a smaller ionic radius** than potassium ions.

When you combine a higher charge with a smaller radius, you get a much higher **charge density**. This means the positive charge is concentrated into a smaller volume, allowing the cations to get closer to the delocalized electrons.

highlights.text: Smaller atomic radius leads to greater charge density

steps.title: Relate attraction to melting point
steps.content:
Because the iron ions have a higher charge and a smaller radius, there is a much **stronger electrostatic attraction** between the $Fe^{n+}$ cations and the delocalized electrons compared to $K^{+}$.

More energy (in the form of heat) is required to overcome these stronger metallic bonds and transition the metal from a solid to a liquid state. This results in iron having a significantly higher melting point.

highlights.text: Stronger electrostatic attraction between cations and electrons in iron

highlights.text: Metals like copper can be drawn into wires

steps.title: State the direct definition
steps.content:
Using the direct technical definition, **ductility** is the property of a material that allows it to be stretched or drawn out into thin wires without breaking. 

In metals, this is possible because the positive metal ions can slide past each other into new positions within the 'sea' of delocalized electrons without the metallic bond snapping.

steps.title: Confirm markscheme requirement
steps.content:
To earn the mark in an IB exam, your definition must specifically mention the ability to be shaped into wires. 

**Definition:** The ability of a material to be drawn into wires.

parts.content: Explain why metallic bonding allows ductility.
parts.markscheme:

steps.title: Identify the property
steps.content: The question asks us to explain ductility, which is the ability of a material to be stretched into a wire. To understand this at the IB level, we look at **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**, which describes metals as a regular lattice of positive ions (cations) surrounded by a "sea" of delocalized electrons.

highlights.text: drawn into wires

steps.title: Explain sliding of layers
steps.content: In a metallic lattice, the positive ions are arranged in a regular, repeating pattern. Because this structure is organized, the layers of metal ions can slide over each other when a force is applied (such as pulling the metal to form a wire).

steps.title: Role of delocalized electrons
steps.content: Unlike ionic bonds, which are rigid and brittle, metallic bonds are non-directional. As the layers of ions slide, the mobile sea of delocalized electrons flows around them. This maintains the electrostatic attraction between the ions and the electrons, preventing the structure from shattering or breaking the metallic bond.

steps.title: Conclusion for marks
steps.content:
To secure full marks on an exam, you must mention both the physical movement and the bonding mechanism:
1. Layers of metal ions slide over each other without breaking the bond.
2. The delocalized sea of electrons maintains the attraction between the ions during this movement.

parts.content: Identify one additional property important for wire applications. Justify your choice.
parts.markscheme:
- Identify a property such as high electrical conductivity or high tensile strength. <marks>A1</marks>
- Justify the choice (e.g., electrical conductivity is needed to carry current, or tensile strength is needed to resist breaking under tension). <marks>R1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Identify the property
steps.content: While ductility allows metals like copper to be formed into wires, the most essential property for their functional use in electrical circuits is **high electrical conductivity**.

highlights.text: Identify a property such as high electrical conductivity

steps.title: Justify the choice
steps.content:
The primary purpose of a wire is to carry an electric current from one point to another. High electrical conductivity is vital because it allows the flow of charge with **minimal energy loss** (in the form of heat). 

In metallic bonding, this is possible due to the "sea of delocalized electrons" that are free to move throughout the lattice when a potential difference is applied. This concept is covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**.

highlights.text: electrical conductivity is needed to carry current

steps.title: Finalize the answer
steps.content:
To earn both marks, clearly state the property and its specific application:

- **Property:** High electrical conductivity. 
- **Justification:** It is required to allow the efficient flow of electric current through the wire.

steps.title: Identify the property
steps.content: To be useful as a wire, a metal must possess **high tensile strength**. This is a measure of how much stress (pulling force) a material can withstand before it breaks.

highlights.text: Identify one additional property important for wire applications

steps.title: Justify the choice
steps.content:
The justification for this choice is practical: during installation, wires are often pulled through narrow conduits or stretched across long distances (like overhead power lines). 

If the metal did not have high tensile strength, it would snap or deform permanently under this **tension**, making it useless for transmitting electricity or providing structural support.

highlights.text: Justify your choice

steps.title: Understand the marking
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn marks by connecting the property to its functional necessity:

*   **1 mark (A1)**: Correctly identifying the property (High tensile strength).
*   **1 mark (R1)**: Providing a valid reason (To resist breaking when under tension or during installation).

steps.title: Identify the group position
steps.content: The question asks about the trend for Group 2 metals: calcium ($\text{Ca}$), strontium ($\text{Sr}$), and barium ($\text{Ba}$). These elements are located in the same vertical column of the periodic table, meaning they share similar chemical properties but have different numbers of electron shells.

highlights.text: move down Group 2
calcium to barium

steps.title: Understand the periodic principle
steps.content:
In **Topic S3.1.3: Periodicity of properties**, we learn that as you move down a group, each successive element has one additional principal energy level (electron shell) compared to the one above it. 

Even though the nuclear charge (number of protons) also increases, the addition of a new inner shell provides a "shielding effect" and places the outermost electrons significantly further from the nucleus.

steps.title: State the final trend
steps.content:
Because each step down the group adds a new electron shell, the distance between the nucleus and the outermost electrons increases. 

Therefore, the atomic radius **increases** as you move down Group 2 from calcium to barium.

highlights.text: atomic radius

steps.title: Identify the elements
steps.content: To determine the trend, we first locate the Group 2 elements mentioned in the question: Calcium ($Ca$), Strontium ($Sr$), and Barium ($Ba$). In the periodic table, these are arranged vertically in Group 2, with Calcium at the top and Barium further down.

highlights.text: calcium, strontium, and barium

steps.title: Consult the Data Booklet
steps.content: To find the specific numerical values for atomic radii, we refer to **Section 9** of the IB Chemistry Data Booklet, titled "Atomic radii". This section provides the calculated atomic radius for each element in units of $10^{-12} \text{ m}$.

steps.title: Extract numerical data
steps.content:
From Section 9, we find the following atomic radius values:

*   **Calcium ($Ca$):** $197 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}$
*   **Strontium ($Sr$):** $215 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}$
*   **Barium ($Ba$):** $222 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}$

steps.title: State the trend
steps.content:
By comparing these values ($197 < 215 < 222$), we can see that the numerical value increases as we move from Calcium to Barium. 

Therefore, the **atomic radius increases** as you move down Group 2.

steps.title: Identify group positions
steps.content: First, we locate Calcium ($Ca$), Strontium ($Sr$), and Barium ($Ba$) on the periodic table. They are all members of **Group 2** (the alkaline earth metals). To predict their melting points, we need to apply the periodic trend for metallic bond strength as we move down a group.

highlights.text: calcium, strontium, and barium

steps.title: Analyze ionic radius trend
steps.content:
As we move down Group 2 from $Ca$ to $Sr$ to $Ba$, the number of occupied electron shells increases. This results in an **increase in the ionic radius** of the metal cations. 

This concept is a prerequisite from **Topic S3.1: Periodicity of properties**.

steps.title: Relate size to bond strength
steps.content:
Metallic bonding is the electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions and a "sea" of delocalized electrons. 

According to **Topic S2.3.2 (Strength of metallic bonds)**, as the ionic radius increases, the delocalized electrons are further from the nucleus. This leads to a **weaker electrostatic attraction**, making the metallic bond easier to break.

highlights.text: strength of their metallic bonding

steps.title: Predict the melting point order
steps.content:
Since melting point is a measure of the energy required to overcome these metallic bonds, a weaker bond results in a lower melting point. Therefore, the melting points decrease down the group:

$$\text{Calcium} > \text{Strontium} > \text{Barium}$$

In chemical symbols, the order is:
$$Ca > Sr > Ba$$

steps.title: Locate elements in Data Booklet
steps.content: To find the specific melting points, we need to refer to **Section 6** of the IB Chemistry Data Booklet, which lists the physical properties of the elements. We are looking for the melting point (measured in Kelvin, $K$) for the Group 2 elements: Calcium ($Ca$), Strontium ($Sr$), and Barium ($Ba$).

steps.title: Extract melting point values
steps.content:
Using the table in Section 6, we find the following values for the melting points:

*   **Calcium ($Ca$):** $1115\text{ K}$
*   **Strontium ($Sr$):** $1050\text{ K}$
*   **Barium ($Ba$):** $1000\text{ K}$

steps.title: Rank in descending order
steps.content:
Comparing these values, we can see that the melting points decrease as we move down Group 2 from Calcium to Barium. This is because the atomic radius increases, which weakens the metallic bond. 

Arranging them from highest to lowest melting point gives us:

$$Ca > Sr > Ba$$

highlights.text: Predict the correct order as $Ca > Sr > Ba$

parts.markscheme:
- Explain that metallic bonding weakens as atomic radius increases down the group. <marks>M1</marks>
- State that the electrostatic attraction between metal cations and delocalized electrons decreases as distance increases. <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the radius trend
steps.content:
As you move down Group 2 from calcium ($Ca$) to barium ($Ba$), the number of occupied electron shells increases. This results in an increase in the **atomic radius** (and ionic radius) of the metal atoms.

This concept is a core part of **Topic S3.1: The periodic table** where we study periodicity and group trends.

steps.title: Relate distance to attraction
steps.content:
Metallic bonding is the electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal cations and a "sea" of delocalized electrons. 

Because the radius of the cations increases down the group, the distance between the positive nucleus and the delocalized valence electrons increases. As the distance ($r$) increases, the strength of the electrostatic attraction decreases, leading to a weaker metallic bond.

$$\text{Metallic Bond Strength} \propto \frac{\text{Charge of Ion}}{\text{Radius of Ion}}$$

highlights.text: metallic bonding

steps.title: Summary of the trend
steps.content:
In summary, down Group 2:
1. The **atomic radius increases** due to additional electron shells.
2. The **electrostatic attraction** between the metal cations and the delocalized electrons decreases because they are further apart.
3. Consequently, the **metallic bond strength weakens**, which typically leads to lower melting points down the group.

This aligns with the requirements of **Topic S2.3.2: Strength of metallic bonds**.

steps.title: Analyze the Periodic Trend
steps.content:
To explain the trend in metallic bond strength, we first look at the physical changes down Group 2. As we move from calcium ($Ca$) to strontium ($Sr$) and barium ($Ba$), the number of occupied electron shells increases. 

This results in an increase in the **atomic and ionic radius** of the metals down the group.

highlights.text: calcium, strontium, and barium

steps.title: Compare Charge and Volume
steps.content:
In Group 2, all metal atoms lose two electrons to form cations with a **constant charge** of $+2$ (e.g., $Ca^{2+}$, $Sr^{2+}$, $Ba^{2+}$).

While the charge remains the same, the **ionic volume** increases significantly as we go down the group due to the additional electron shells.

steps.title: Evaluate Cation Charge Density
steps.content:
The **charge density** of a cation is the ratio of its charge to its volume. 

$$\text{Charge Density} \propto \frac{\text{Ionic Charge}}{\text{Ionic Volume}}$$

Because the charge is constant but the volume increases, the **cation charge density decreases** as we move down Group 2.

steps.title: Link to Bond Strength
steps.content:
Metallic bonding is the electrostatic attraction between positive cations and the 'sea' of delocalized electrons. 

A lower charge density and a larger ionic radius mean that the delocalized electrons are, on average, further away from the positive nuclei. This leads to a **weaker electrostatic attraction**, which results in a **weaker metallic bond** down the group.

**Marking Points:**
*   **M1:** Metallic bonding weakens as atomic radius increases down the group.
*   **A1:** Electrostatic attraction between metal cations and delocalized electrons decreases as distance increases.

highlights.text: metallic bonding

parts.content: State how ionic charge changes across the period.
parts.markscheme: Increases: $+1$, $+2$, $+3$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify groups for each element
steps.content:
To determine the ionic charges, we first look at the position of each element in the periodic table. For the elements in Period 3, we find:

*   **Sodium (Na)** is in **Group 1**.
*   **Magnesium (Mg)** is in **Group 2**.
*   **Aluminium (Al)** is in **Group 13**.

highlights.text: Sodium, magnesium, and aluminium are in Period 3.

steps.title: Relate group to ionic charge
steps.content:
Main group metals typically lose their valence electrons to achieve a stable noble gas electron configuration. The number of electrons lost matches the group number (for Groups 1 and 2) or the last digit of the group number (for Group 13):

*   **Group 1** elements lose $1$ electron to form ions with a **$+1$** charge (e.g., $\text{Na}^{+}$).
*   **Group 2** elements lose $2$ electrons to form ions with a **$+2$** charge (e.g., $\text{Mg}^{2+}$).
*   **Group 13** elements lose $3$ electrons to form ions with a **$+3$** charge (e.g., $\text{Al}^{3+}$).

steps.title: State the increasing trend
steps.content:
As we move from left to right across Period 3 from sodium to aluminium, the number of valence electrons increases, leading to a corresponding increase in the charge of the ions formed.

The ionic charge **increases** from **$+1$** to **$+2$** to **$+3$**.

steps.title: Analyze Valence Electron Configurations
steps.content:
To understand the ionic charge, we first look at the valence electron configurations of these elements in Period 3. Metals aim to reach a stable noble gas configuration by losing their outer-shell electrons. 

This involves the $3s$ and $3p$ subshells, as covered in **S1.3: Electron configurations**. 

*   **Sodium (Na)**: Atomic number 11. Configuration is $[Ne] 3s^1$. It has **1** valence electron.
*   **Magnesium (Mg)**: Atomic number 12. Configuration is $[Ne] 3s^2$. It has **2** valence electrons.
*   **Aluminium (Al)**: Atomic number 13. Configuration is $[Ne] 3s^2 3p^1$. It has **3** valence electrons.

highlights.text: Sodium, magnesium, and aluminium

steps.title: Determine Charges from Electron Loss
steps.content:
When these atoms form ions, they lose all electrons in their $3s$ and $3p$ subshells to achieve the stable configuration of Neon ($[Ne]$). 

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Na} &\rightarrow \text{Na}^+ + e^- && (\text{loses 1 electron} \rightarrow +1 \text{ charge}) \\ \text{Mg} &\rightarrow \text{Mg}^{2+} + 2e^- && (\text{loses 2 electrons} \rightarrow +2 \text{ charge}) \\ \text{Al} &\rightarrow \text{Al}^{3+} + 3e^- && (\text{loses 3 electrons} \rightarrow +3 \text{ charge}) \end{aligned}$$

This follows the principles of ion formation in **S2.1: The ionic model**.

steps.title: Identify the Trend
steps.content:
As we move from sodium to magnesium to aluminium, the number of valence electrons increases, leading to an increase in the positive charge of the ions formed. 

The ionic charge increases from **$+1$** to **$+2$** to **$+3$**.

highlights.text: State how ionic charge changes across the period.

parts.content: Compare their melting points.
parts.markscheme: $\mathrm{Na} < \mathrm{Mg} < \mathrm{Al}$ <marks>A1</marks>

steps.title: Identify the bonding type
steps.content:
Sodium, magnesium, and aluminium are all metals. Their physical properties, such as melting point, are determined by the strength of **metallic bonding**. This is the electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions and a 'sea' of delocalized valence electrons. 

This concept is covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model** of your syllabus.

highlights.text: Sodium, magnesium, and aluminium

steps.title: Compare delocalized electrons
steps.content:
The strength of the metallic bond depends partly on the number of delocalized electrons. As we move across Period 3 from sodium to aluminium, the number of valence electrons increases:

- **Na** ($[Ne] 3s^1$) provides **1** delocalized electron per atom.
- **Mg** ($[Ne] 3s^2$) provides **2** delocalized electrons per atom.
- **Al** ($[Ne] 3s^2 3p^1$) provides **3** delocalized electrons per atom.

A higher density of delocalized electrons leads to a stronger electrostatic attraction within the lattice.

steps.title: Analyze ionic radius
steps.content:
The strength of the bond also depends on the **ionic radius**. As we move from $Na$ to $Al$:
1. The number of protons increases (increasing nuclear charge).
2. The electrons are added to the same valence shell, so shielding remains relatively constant.
3. The result is a stronger pull on the electrons, making the ionic radius decrease: $$r(Na^+) > r(Mg^{2+}) > r(Al^{3+})$$

Smaller ions allow the delocalized electrons to get closer to the positive nuclei, significantly strengthening the metallic bond.

steps.title: Deduce melting point order
steps.content:
By combining these factors, we can see that aluminium has the strongest metallic bonds (most delocalized electrons and smallest ionic radius), while sodium has the weakest. 

Stronger bonds require more thermal energy to break, resulting in higher melting points. Therefore, the order of increasing melting points is:

$$\mathrm{Na} < \mathrm{Mg} < \mathrm{Al}$$

highlights.text: melting points

steps.title: Consult the Data Booklet
steps.content: To compare the melting points of sodium, magnesium, and aluminium using the most direct evidence, we should consult the **IB Chemistry Data Booklet**. Specifically, look for the table titled **"Physical properties of the elements"**, which lists melting points in Kelvin ($K$).

steps.title: Identify melting point values
steps.content:
From the physical properties table, we can find the specific melting point values for each Period 3 metal:

- **Sodium (Na):** $371 \text{ K}$
- **Magnesium (Mg):** $923 \text{ K}$
- **Aluminium (Al):** $933 \text{ K}$

steps.title: Compare and rank
steps.content:
By comparing these numerical values, we can see that sodium has the lowest melting point and aluminium has the highest. 

$$\begin{aligned} 371 \text{ K} &< 923 \text{ K} < 933 \text{ K} \\ \text{Na} &< \text{Mg} < \text{Al} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The relative order of their melting points is:

$$\mathrm{Na} < \mathrm{Mg} < \mathrm{Al}$$

steps.title: Identify the elements
steps.content: Sodium ($Na$), magnesium ($Mg$), and aluminium ($Al$) are the first three elements in **Period 3**. They are all metals, which means their physical properties, like melting point, are determined by the strength of their **metallic bonding**.

highlights.text: Sodium, magnesium, and aluminium are in Period 3.

steps.title: Analyze metallic bond strength
steps.content:
Metallic bonding is the electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions and a 'sea' of delocalized electrons. According to periodic trends covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**, the strength of this bond increases across the period because:

1.  **Ionic Charge increases**: $Na$ forms $1+$ ions, $Mg$ forms $2+$ ions, and $Al$ forms $3+$ ions.
2.  **Delocalized Electrons increase**: Each atom contributes more electrons to the delocalized sea ($Na=1, Mg=2, Al=3$).
3.  **Ionic Radius decreases**: The ions get smaller across the period, allowing the delocalized electrons to be closer to the nuclei.

steps.title: Determine the melting trend
steps.content: Because the metallic bond becomes significantly stronger as we move from sodium to aluminium, more energy is required to overcome these attractions and melt the metal. Therefore, the melting point increases across these three elements.

steps.title: State the final comparison
steps.content:
Following the trend of increasing bond strength, the correct order for the melting points is:

$$\mathrm{Na} < \mathrm{Mg} < \mathrm{Al}$$

steps.title: Define metallic bonding
steps.content: To explain the trend, we first recall that metallic bonding is the electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions (cations) and a "sea" of delocalized valence electrons. This is covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**.

steps.title: Analyze delocalized electrons
steps.content:
As we move across Period 3 from sodium to aluminium, the number of valence electrons increases:

*   **Sodium (Na):** 1 valence electron becomes delocalized.
*   **Magnesium (Mg):** 2 valence electrons become delocalized.
*   **Aluminium (Al):** 3 valence electrons become delocalized.

An increase in the number of delocalized electrons per atom contributes to a stronger electrostatic attraction.

highlights.text: More delocalized electrons per atom

steps.title: Analyze ionic radius
steps.content:
Across the period, the nuclear charge increases (from $+11$ to $+13$), which pulls the electrons closer to the nucleus. Consequently, the ionic radius decreases from $\text{Na}^+$ to $\text{Al}^{3+}$.

A **smaller ionic radius** combined with a **higher ionic charge** significantly increases the **charge density** of the cations.

steps.title: Conclude on bonding strength
steps.content:
Because the cations have a higher charge density and there are more delocalized electrons, the electrostatic attraction between them becomes much stronger. 

Therefore, the **strength of the metallic bond increases** across the period from sodium to aluminium.

steps.title: Identify metallic bonding components
steps.content: To explain the trend in Period 3 (Na, Mg, Al), we first need to recall the nature of metallic bonding. It is the electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions (cations) and a "sea" of delocalized valence electrons. This concept is covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**.

steps.title: Compare delocalized electrons
steps.content:
As we move from sodium to aluminium, the number of valence electrons increases. This means each atom contributes more electrons to the delocalized sea:

*   **Sodium (Na):** 1 delocalized electron per atom ($Na^{+}$)
*   **Magnesium (Mg):** 2 delocalized electrons per atom ($Mg^{2+}$)
*   **Aluminium (Al):** 3 delocalized electrons per atom ($Al^{3+}$)

This increase in the number of delocalized electrons is the first factor that strengthens the bond.

highlights.text: More delocalized electrons per atom

steps.title: Analyze charge density
steps.content:
The **Charge Density Approach** looks at the ratio of ionic charge to ionic radius. Across Period 3 from Na to Al:

1.  **Ionic charge increases:** from $1+$ to $3+$.
2.  **Ionic radius decreases:** because the increasing nuclear charge (more protons) pulls the remaining electrons closer.

Since charge density is essentially $\text{charge} / \text{radius}$, having a higher charge and a smaller radius results in a much higher **charge density** for the $Al^{3+}$ ion compared to $Na^{+}$.

highlights.text: Smaller radius increases charge density

steps.title: Relate to bonding strength
steps.content:
Because the $Al^{3+}$ ions have a higher charge density and there are more delocalized electrons, the electrostatic attraction between the cations and the electron sea is much stronger than in sodium.

steps.title: Define malleability
steps.content:
In chemistry, **malleability** is defined as the ability of a substance to be reshaped into thin sheets through hammering or rolling without breaking or cracking.

To earn the mark in an IB exam, you should focus on the specific outcome (forming sheets) and the method (hammering or rolling).

highlights.text: Ability to be hammered or rolled into sheets

This property is a direct result of metallic bonding (covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**), where layers of metal cations can slide over each other without breaking the 'sea' of delocalized electrons.

steps.title: Define the metallic lattice
steps.content:
To understand malleability, we first look at the structure of a metal like aluminium. In the metallic model, positive metal ions (cations) are arranged in a regular, 3D lattice. These ions are surrounded by a "sea" of delocalized electrons. 

This structure is covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model** of your syllabus.

steps.title: Describe the sliding layers
steps.content: The key to malleability is how these ions are organized. They are arranged in regular **layers**. When a mechanical force is applied (like when aluminium is rolled into foil), these layers of positive ions can **slide over one another** without the structure falling apart.

highlights.text: Layers of positive ions slide over one another

steps.title: Explain bond maintenance
steps.content: Unlike ionic bonds, which are rigid and brittle, metallic bonding is **non-directional**. As the layers shift, the **delocalized electrons** are free to move with them. This ensures that the electrostatic attraction between the cations and the electrons is constantly maintained, preventing the metal from breaking.

highlights.text: Delocalized electrons maintain bonding

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must mention two distinct points:
1. The **layers of positive ions** can **slide** over each other when pressure is applied.
2. The **delocalized electrons** move to **maintain the metallic bond** throughout the movement, allowing the metal to change shape rather than shatter.

steps.title: Recall the metallic model
steps.content: To understand malleability, we first look at the structure of a metal. Metals consist of a regular lattice of positive metal ions (cations) surrounded by a "sea" of delocalized valence electrons. This is the foundation of **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**.

steps.title: Describe the sliding layers
steps.content: When a physical force is applied to a metal (like Aluminium), the regular arrangement allows the **layers of positive ions to slide over one another**. Unlike in an ionic crystal, where sliding would bring like-charges together and cause repulsion, the metallic structure is more flexible.

highlights.text: Layers of positive ions slide over one another

steps.title: Apply non-directional bonding
steps.content: The key to why the metal doesn't break is the **non-directional** nature of the bond. The electrostatic attraction exists between the cations and the sea of delocalized electrons in all directions. As the ions move, the **delocalized electrons maintain the bonding** by quickly adjusting their positions to surround the cations in their new locations.

highlights.text: Delocalized electrons maintain bonding

steps.title: Conclude on malleability
steps.content: Because the electrostatic attraction is never interrupted during the sliding of layers, the metal can be deformed into different shapes (malleability) without fracturing. This makes Aluminium perfect for thin products like kitchen foil.

steps.title: Compare delocalized electrons
steps.content:
According to the provided data, magnesium has **2** delocalized electrons per atom, while lithium only has **1**. This is the first key factor in determining the strength of the metallic bond.

This concept is covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model** of your syllabus.

highlights.text: Number of Delocalized Electrons

steps.title: Identify metal cation charges
steps.content:
Metallic bonding consists of a lattice of positive metal cations surrounded by a 'sea' of delocalized electrons. Based on the number of valence electrons lost to the delocalized sea:

- Lithium (Group 1) forms $Li^{+}$ cations with a **$1+$** charge.
- Magnesium (Group 2) forms $Mg^{2+}$ cations with a **$2+$** charge.

The higher the charge on the cation, the greater the potential for attraction.

steps.title: Analyze electrostatic attraction
steps.content:
Metallic bond strength is determined by the electrostatic attraction between the positive cations and the delocalized electrons. 

Because Magnesium has a higher cation charge ($2+$ vs $1+$) and a greater density of delocalized electrons ($2$ vs $1$ per atom), the electrostatic attraction within the magnesium lattice is significantly stronger than in the lithium lattice.

steps.title: Deduce the stronger bond
steps.content:
Therefore, **magnesium (Mg) has stronger metallic bonding** than lithium (Li).

To earn full marks on an IB exam, ensure you mention both the specific number of electrons and the resulting cation charges to justify the increased attraction.

steps.title: Compare valence electrons and charge
steps.content:
To understand why magnesium is structurally superior, we first look at their positions in the periodic table. Lithium is a Group 1 metal, while magnesium is in Group 2. 

In a metallic lattice, atoms lose their valence electrons to form a "sea of delocalized electrons." 
- **Lithium** forms $Li^{+}$ ions and contributes **one** electron per atom to the sea.
- **Magnesium** forms $Mg^{2+}$ ions and contributes **two** electrons per atom to the sea.

This is a core concept from **Topic 2.3: The metallic model**.

steps.title: Evaluate metallic bond strength
steps.content:
The strength of a metallic bond depends on the electrostatic attraction between the positive metal ions and the delocalized electrons. 

Because magnesium has a higher ionic charge ($2+$ vs $1+$) and more delocalized electrons, the electrostatic attraction in magnesium is much stronger than in lithium. This leads to a more tightly bound and rigid lattice structure.

steps.title: Link bonding to mechanical properties
steps.content:
For the first mark (**M1**), we must link this bonding strength to physical properties. Because the metallic bonds in magnesium are stronger, the metal is significantly **harder** and **stiffer** than lithium. 

In aerospace engineering, "stiffness" (resistance to elastic deformation) is critical for maintaining the shape of a structural component under heavy loads.

highlights.text: greater mechanical strength, hardness, and stiffness

steps.title: Consider stability and reactivity
steps.content:
For the second mark (**R1**), we consider environmental stability. Magnesium is generally less chemically reactive than lithium (which is highly reactive and can even ignite in air more easily). 

Additionally, the stronger bonds in magnesium result in a **higher melting point**. These factors ensure that the material remains structurally stable and safe in the extreme environments typical of aerospace applications.

highlights.text: less chemically reactive
higher melting point

steps.title: Analyze the requirements
steps.content: To answer why magnesium ($Mg$) is preferred for structural aerospace applications over lithium ($Li$), we must look at two main factors: its mechanical strength (derived from metallic bonding) and its stability in extreme environments (thermal and chemical properties). Even though lithium is lighter, its physical and chemical properties make it unsuitable as a primary structural material.

highlights.text: suitable than lithium in structural applications

steps.title: Evaluate metallic bonding strength
steps.content:
According to **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**, the strength of a metallic bond depends on the charge of the metal ion and the number of delocalized electrons. 

$$\begin{aligned} &\text{Lithium (Group 1): } Li^+ \text{ ions, } 1 \text{ delocalized } e^- \text{ per atom} \\ &\text{Magnesium (Group 2): } Mg^{2+} \text{ ions, } 2 \text{ delocalized } e^- \text{ per atom} \end{aligned}$$

Because magnesium has a higher ionic charge and more delocalized electrons, it forms **stronger metallic bonds**. This results in significantly greater mechanical strength, hardness, and stiffness, which are essential for supporting the loads of an aircraft.

steps.title: Assess thermal and chemical stability
steps.content:
In aerospace environments, materials face high temperatures (from friction and engines) and corrosive conditions. 

1.  **Thermal Stability:** The stronger metallic bonds in magnesium lead to a much **higher melting point** than lithium. Magnesium remains solid and structurally sound at temperatures where lithium would soften or melt.
2.  **Chemical Stability:** Following trends in **Topic S3.1: Trends in chemical reactivity**, lithium (an alkali metal) is highly reactive and oxidizes rapidly in air. Magnesium is **less chemically reactive**, providing better resistance to environmental degradation.

This combination of higher thermal resistance and lower reactivity ensures the structural integrity of the alloy during flight.

steps.title: Final answer and marking summary
steps.content:
To earn full marks, you must mention both the bonding/strength aspect and the stability aspect:

*   **M1:** Magnesium forms stronger metallic bonds, which provides the necessary mechanical strength and stiffness for structures.
*   **R1:** Magnesium is less chemically reactive and has a higher melting point than lithium, ensuring it stays stable in harsh aerospace environments.

steps.title: Identify the element positions
steps.content: Sodium ($\text{Na}$), magnesium ($\text{Mg}$), and aluminium ($\text{Al}$) are the first three elements in Period 3. This question asks us to identify the general periodic trends as we move from left to right across this period. This concept is covered in **Topic S3.1.3: Periodicity of properties**.

highlights.text: Na, Mg, and Al across Period 3

steps.title: Describe nuclear charge trend
steps.content:
As we move from $\text{Na}$ to $\text{Al}$, the **nuclear charge increases**. This is because the atomic number increases by one for each element (from $11$ to $13$), resulting in an increasing number of protons in the nucleus.

Stating that the nuclear charge increases due to more protons earns you the first mark (**A1**).

highlights.text: nuclear charge increases due to the increasing number of protons

steps.title: Describe atomic radius trend
steps.content:
The **atomic radius decreases** from $\text{Na}$ to $\text{Al}$. 

This happens because, while the number of occupied electron shells remains the same (the 3rd shell), the increasing nuclear charge exerts a stronger electrostatic attraction on the valence electrons, pulling them closer to the nucleus and reducing the overall size of the atom.

Identifying that the radius decreases earns you the second mark (**M1**).

highlights.text: atomic radius decreases from Na to Al

steps.title: Define Nuclear Charge
steps.content:
Nuclear charge refers to the total charge of the nucleus, which is determined by the number of protons it contains. In the Periodic Table, this corresponds to the **atomic number ($Z$)** of the element.

$$\text{Nuclear Charge} = \text{Number of Protons} = Z$$

steps.title: Analyze Nuclear Charge Data
steps.content:
Using the **Periodic Table** in the Data Booklet, we find the atomic numbers for the three elements in Period 3:

*   **Sodium ($\text{Na}$):** $Z = 11$
*   **Magnesium ($\text{Mg}$):** $Z = 12$
*   **Aluminium ($\text{Al}$):** $Z = 13$

Comparing these values, the **nuclear charge increases** as we move from $\text{Na}$ to $\text{Al}$ across the period.

*   **Sodium ($\text{Na}$):** $186\text{ pm}$
*   **Magnesium ($\text{Mg}$):** $160\text{ pm}$
*   **Aluminium ($\text{Al}$):** $143\text{ pm}$

As we move from $\text{Na}$ to $\text{Al}$, the numerical values show that the **atomic radius decreases**.

steps.title: Recall periodic trends
steps.content:
To answer this, we first look at the trends across the period from $\text{Na}$ to $\text{Al}$. This topic is covered in **S3.1 Periodicity**. 

As we move from sodium to aluminium:
1. The **nuclear charge increases** because the number of protons increases ($\text{Na} = 11$, $\text{Mg} = 12$, $\text{Al} = 13$).
2. The **atomic radius decreases** because the increased nuclear charge pulls the electrons closer to the nucleus.

highlights.text: change in atomic radius and nuclear charge

steps.title: Determine charge density
steps.content:
In metallic bonding, atoms release their valence electrons to form a 'sea' of delocalized electrons. 

Across Period 3:
- $\text{Na}$ forms $\text{Na}^+$
- $\text{Mg}$ forms $\text{Mg}^{2+}$
- $\text{Al}$ forms $\text{Al}^{3+}$

Because the ions have a **higher nuclear charge** and a **smaller atomic radius**, they have a much **higher charge density**. This is the first key point for earning marks in the IB markscheme.

steps.title: Analyze electrostatic attraction
steps.content:
Metallic bond strength is determined by the electrostatic attraction between the positive metal ions and the delocalized electrons. 

Since the ions in $\text{Al}$ have a higher charge and smaller radius compared to $\text{Na}$, they exert a **stronger electrostatic pull** on the sea of delocalized electrons. 

This stronger attraction makes the metallic bond significantly stronger as you move from $\text{Na}$ to $\text{Al}$.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
To get full marks, ensure your explanation links the structure to the force:

1.  **M1:** State that the smaller atomic radius and higher nuclear charge (and higher ionic charge) lead to an **increased charge density** of the metal ions.
2.  **A1:** Explain that this results in **stronger electrostatic attraction** between the metal ions and the sea of delocalized electrons.

steps.title: Identify the Periodic Trends
steps.content:
To understand the strength of metallic bonding, we first look at how the atoms change from sodium ($\text{Na}$) to aluminium ($\text{Al}$) across Period 3. This topic is part of **Topic 3.1: The Periodic Table**. 

As we move from $\text{Na}$ to $\text{Al}$:
- The **nuclear charge increases** (from $+11$ in $\text{Na}$ to $+13$ in $\text{Al}$).
- The **atomic (and ionic) radius decreases** because the increasing nuclear charge pulls the electron shells closer to the nucleus.
- The number of **delocalized electrons** increases ($1$ for $\text{Na}^{+}$, $2$ for $\text{Mg}^{2+}$, and $3$ for $\text{Al}^{3+}$).

highlights.text: atomic radius and nuclear charge

steps.title: Explain Charge Density
steps.content:
The **Charge Density Approach** focuses on the ratio of an ion's charge to its size. 

Across the period, the metal ions have a higher positive charge (e.g., $\text{Al}^{3+}$ vs $\text{Na}^{+}$) and a smaller radius. Because the charge is concentrated into a smaller volume, we say the **charge density** of the metal ion increases significantly from sodium to aluminium.

In an IB exam, mentioning that a **smaller atomic radius and higher nuclear charge leads to increased charge density** is essential for the first mark.

steps.title: Relate to Bonding Strength
steps.content:
Metallic bonding is defined in **Topic 2.3: The Metallic Model** as the electrostatic attraction between the lattice of positive metal ions and the "sea" of delocalized valence electrons.

Because $\text{Al}^{3+}$ has a much higher charge density and more delocalized electrons than $\text{Na}^{+}$, there is a **stronger electrostatic attraction** between the ions and the delocalized electrons. 

This increased attraction results in a stronger metallic bond, which explains why aluminium has a much higher melting point than sodium.

Metallic bonding consists of a lattice of positive metal ions surrounded by a "sea" of delocalized valence electrons. As we move from $\text{Na}$ to $\text{Al}$, the number of valence electrons contributed to this sea increases:

*   **Sodium ($\text{Na}$)**: 1 valence electron ($3s^1$)
*   **Magnesium ($\text{Mg}$)**: 2 valence electrons ($3s^2$)
*   **Aluminium ($\text{Al}$)**: 3 valence electrons ($3s^2 3p^1$)

This is covered in **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**.

highlights.text: sodium (Na), magnesium (Mg), and aluminium (Al)

steps.title: Determine ionic charge
steps.content:
When these atoms lose their valence electrons to form the metallic lattice, the charge of the resulting metal ions increases:

$$\text{Na}^+ < \text{Mg}^{2+} < \text{Al}^{3+}$$

A higher ionic charge creates a stronger electrostatic attraction between the positive ions and the delocalized electrons.

steps.title: Relate to bond strength
steps.content:
According to **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**, the strength of a metallic bond depends on the charge density and the number of delocalized electrons. 

Because $\text{Al}^{3+}$ has more delocalized electrons and a higher charge than $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ and $\text{Na}^+$, the metallic lattice in aluminium is much stronger and requires significantly more energy to break.

steps.title: Conclude on melting points
steps.content:
Since more energy is required to overcome the stronger metallic bonds in the lattice, the melting point increases from $\text{Na}$ to $\text{Al}$.

**Final Answer Summary:**
*   Trend: Increasing melting points.
*   Order: $\text{Na} < \text{Mg} < \text{Al}$.
*   Reason: Increasing ionic charge and number of delocalized electrons strengthen the metallic bond.

steps.title: Reference the Data Booklet
steps.content:
To determine the trend, we first look at the melting point values in the IB Chemistry Data Booklet. Under **Section 6: Physical constants of the elements**, we find the melting points ($T_m$) for these Period 3 metals:

*   **Sodium (Na):** $371\text{ K}$
*   **Magnesium (Mg):** $923\text{ K}$
*   **Aluminium (Al):** $933\text{ K}$

From these values, we can see a clear increase in melting point across the group: $\text{Na} < \text{Mg} < \text{Al}$.

steps.title: Analyze metallic bonding strength
steps.content:
The increase in melting point is due to the increasing strength of the **metallic bond**. Metallic bonding is the electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions and a 'sea' of delocalized valence electrons. 

As we move from Na to Al:
1.  **Number of delocalized electrons increases:** Na has 1, Mg has 2, and Al has 3 valence electrons per atom.
2.  **Ionic charge increases:** The cations produced are $\text{Na}^+$, $\text{Mg}^{2+}$, and $\text{Al}^{3+}$.
3.  **Ionic radius decreases:** The higher nuclear charge pulls the electrons closer, making the cation smaller.

Because Al has the highest charge and the most delocalized electrons, its metallic lattice is held together most strongly.

steps.title: Explain energy requirements
steps.content: Melting requires overcoming the forces holding the lattice together. Since the attraction between the $\text{Al}^{3+}$ ions and the delocalized electrons is significantly stronger than the attraction in $\text{Mg}$ or $\text{Na}$, much more thermal energy is required to break the lattice structure in Aluminium.

highlights.text: stronger metallic bonding requires more energy to break the lattice structure

steps.title: State the final trend
steps.content:
Based on the physical data and chemical reasoning, the trend is an increase in melting points in the following order:

$$\text{Na} < \text{Mg} < \text{Al}$$

highlights.text: melting points increase in the order: Na < Mg < Al

steps.title: Identify valence electrons
steps.content:
To understand the strength of metallic bonds, we first look at the number of valence electrons each metal has. These are the electrons in the outermost shell that become **delocalized** in a metallic lattice.

By looking at their groups in the periodic table (Topic S3.1.2):
- **Sodium (Na)** is in Group 1, so it has $1$ valence electron.
- **Magnesium (Mg)** is in Group 2, so it has $2$ valence electrons.
- **Aluminium (Al)** is in Group 13, so it has $3$ valence electrons.

In a metal, these valence electrons are shared among all the atoms, creating a "sea of delocalized electrons."

steps.title: Compare delocalized electron density
steps.content:
As we move from $\text{Na}$ to $\text{Mg}$ to $\text{Al}$, each atom contributes more electrons to the delocalized pool. This increases the **electron density** within the lattice.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Na} &\rightarrow 1 \text{ delocalized } e^{-} \text{ per atom} \\ \text{Mg} &\rightarrow 2 \text{ delocalized } e^{-} \text{ per atom} \\ \text{Al} &\rightarrow 3 \text{ delocalized } e^{-} \text{ per atom} \end{aligned}$$

This is the first mark ($M1$) in the markscheme: identifying that $\text{Mg}$ and $\text{Al}$ provide more delocalized electrons than $\text{Na}$.

highlights.text: State that Mg and Al contribute more delocalized electrons per atom (Mg = 2, Al = 3) than Na (1)

steps.title: Relate density to strength
steps.content:
Metallic bonding is the **electrostatic attraction** between the lattice of positive metal ions (cations) and the sea of delocalized electrons. 

When there are more delocalized electrons:
1. The charge on the metal cation increases (e.g., $\text{Al}^{3+}$ vs $\text{Na}^{+}$).
2. There is a higher density of negative charge between the cations.

This results in a much stronger electrostatic attraction, making the metallic bond stronger. This earns the second mark ($A1$).

highlights.text: Conclude that more delocalized electrons strengthen the metallic bonds by increasing the electron density in the lattice

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
In summary, because aluminium contributes three delocalized electrons compared to sodium's one, it has a significantly higher electron density and a stronger metallic bond.

$$\text{Bond Strength: } \text{Na} < \text{Mg} < \text{Al}$$

steps.title: Define the Metallic Bond
steps.content:
To understand why aluminium is so useful, we first look at its internal structure. Metallic bonding is defined as the strong electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions and a 'sea' of delocalized electrons. 

In the case of aluminium ($Z=13$), each atom contributes three valence electrons to this delocalized sea, resulting in a lattice of $Al^{3+}$ ions. You can review the basics of this in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**.

steps.title: Link Bonding to Strength
steps.content:
The mechanical strength of a metal depends on the strength of these electrostatic attractions. Because aluminium forms $Al^{3+}$ ions, it has a higher charge and more delocalized electrons per atom compared to sodium ($Na^+$) or magnesium ($Mg^{2+}$).

This results in a very high charge density and a stronger pull between the ions and electrons, explaining why the metallic bonding in Al provides high mechanical strength.

steps.title: Optimizing Aerospace Alloys
steps.content:
In material science, we don't just use pure metals; we design alloys to meet specific needs. Understanding the fundamental bonding in aluminium allows engineers to perform the optimization of alloys. 

By introducing different sized atoms into the aluminium lattice (as discussed in **Topic S2.4.3: Alloys**), they can prevent layers from sliding while maintaining a low density. This chemical 'fine-tuning' is essential for creating materials that are both lightweight and durable for the extreme conditions of aerospace travel.

Calcium has an atomic number of 20. We can write its electron configuration to find the valence electrons:

- **Full configuration:** $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^2$
- **Condensed configuration:** $[Ar] 4s^2$

The outermost shell ($n=4$) contains **2 electrons**. Therefore, Calcium contributes 2 delocalized electrons.

Aluminium has an atomic number of 13. Let's look at its electron configuration:

- **Full configuration:** $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^1$
- **Condensed configuration:** $[Ne] 3s^2 3p^1$

The outermost shell ($n=3$) contains $2 + 1 =$ **3 electrons**. Therefore, Aluminium contributes 3 delocalized electrons.

steps.title: Define Metallic Bonding
steps.content:
To understand the factors affecting bond strength, we first need to recall what a metallic bond is. In a metal, atoms lose their valence electrons to form a lattice of positive ions (cations) surrounded by a 'sea' of delocalized electrons. The metallic bond is the **electrostatic attraction** between these cations and the delocalized electrons. 

You can review the basics of this model in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**.

steps.title: Factor 1: Ionic Charge
steps.content:
The first major factor is the **charge of the metal ion**. 

As the positive charge on the metal cation increases, the electrostatic attraction between the cation and the delocalized electrons becomes significantly stronger. For example, a $Mg^{2+}$ ion will have a stronger pull on the electron sea than a $Na^{+}$ ion, leading to a higher melting point and a stronger bond.

steps.title: Factor 2: Ionic Radius
steps.content:
The second factor is the **ionic radius** (the size of the cation).

When the ionic radius is smaller, the delocalized electrons are closer to the positive nucleus of the cation. This results in a higher **charge density**, which creates a much stronger electrostatic attraction. 

Generally, as you move across a period, the ionic radius decreases while the charge increases, both of which strengthen the metallic bond.

steps.title: Factor 3: Delocalized Electrons
steps.content:
The final factor is the **number of delocalized electrons per atom**.

Metals that have more valence electrons contribute more electrons to the 'sea.' A higher density of delocalized electrons results in a stronger 'glue' holding the lattice together. 

For instance, Aluminum (Group 13) contributes three delocalized electrons per atom, whereas Sodium (Group 1) only contributes one. This makes the metallic bonding in Aluminum much stronger.

steps.title: Explain metallic bond strength
steps.content: Metallic bonding is defined as the **electrostatic attraction** between a lattice of positive metal ions (cations) and a **sea of delocalized electrons**. We use the concept of **charge density** to describe how concentrated the cation's positive charge is and how strongly it pulls on these electrons.

steps.title: Role of Ionic Charge
steps.content: The first factor is the **ionic charge**. As the charge of the metal cation increases (for example, from $Na^{+}$ to $Mg^{2+}$ to $Al^{3+}$), the electrostatic pull on the delocalized electrons becomes stronger. Additionally, atoms with a higher charge contribute more delocalized electrons to the 'sea', which further strengthens the bond.

highlights.text: State that a higher ionic charge results in a stronger electrostatic attraction between the metal cations and the sea of delocalized electrons.
State that an increase in the number of delocalized electrons per atom leads to stronger metallic bonding.

steps.title: Focus on Charge Density
steps.content:
The second factor is the **ionic radius**. **Charge density** is the ratio of the ion's charge to its volume. 

$$\text{Charge Density} \propto \frac{\text{Ionic Charge}}{\text{Ionic Radius}^3}$$

A **smaller ionic radius** results in a higher charge density. This is because the positive charge is concentrated in a smaller volume, allowing the delocalized electrons to get closer to the nucleus of the cation. Following Coulomb's Law, a smaller distance ($r$) between the charges leads to a much stronger attractive force ($F \propto 1/r^2$).

highlights.text: Explain that a smaller ionic radius leads to a higher charge density and a stronger attraction to the delocalized electrons.

steps.title: Summary of bond strength
steps.content: In summary, metallic bond strength increases when there is a **higher ionic charge**, a **greater number of delocalized electrons**, and a **smaller ionic radius** (leading to high charge density). These factors all maximize the electrostatic attraction within the metal lattice.

steps.title: Analyze electron contribution

*   **Sodium (Na):** Group 1 element, so it contributes **1** delocalized electron per atom, leaving behind a **$\text{Na}^{+}$** cation.
*   **Magnesium (Mg):** Group 2 element, contributing **2** delocalized electrons per atom, leaving a **$\text{Mg}^{2+}$** cation.

steps.title: Assess attraction strength
steps.content:
Metallic bonding is the electrostatic attraction between the positive metal cations and the delocalized electrons. The strength of this bond depends on two main factors:
1.  **The charge of the cation:** A higher charge (like $3+$) attracts electrons more strongly than a lower charge (like $1+$).
2.  **The density of the electron sea:** More delocalized electrons lead to a stronger "glue" holding the lattice together.

Aluminum has the highest ionic charge ($3+$) and the most delocalized electrons per atom, resulting in the strongest metallic bonds.

highlights.text: Al has a higher ionic charge (3+) and more delocalized electrons (3) than Na (1+)

steps.title: Connect to melting point

Melting involves providing enough thermal energy to overcome the attractive forces within the metallic lattice. Because the metallic bonds in Aluminum are the strongest among the three, it requires the most energy to break, leading to the highest melting point.

highlights.text: Identify the trend in melting points as $\text{Al} > \text{Mg} > \text{Na}$

steps.title: Analyze ionic charges
steps.content:
Metallic bonding is the electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions and a 'sea' of delocalized valence electrons. Let's look at the ions formed by each metal:

- **Sodium (Na):** forms $\text{Na}^+$ ions and contributes $1$ delocalized electron per atom.
- **Magnesium (Mg):** forms $\text{Mg}^{2+}$ ions and contributes $2$ delocalized electrons per atom.
- **Aluminum (Al):** forms $\text{Al}^{3+}$ ions and contributes $3$ delocalized electrons per atom.

This increase in charge is the first factor that strengthens the bond.

highlights.text: $\text{Al}$ has a higher ionic charge ($3+$) and more delocalized electrons (3) than $\text{Na}$ ($1+$)

steps.title: Evaluate charge density
steps.content:
As we move from $\text{Na}$ to $\text{Al}$, the ionic radius also **decreases**. This is because the increasing nuclear charge (more protons) pulls the electron shells closer to the nucleus.

When we combine the increasing charge with the decreasing radius, we see a significant increase in **charge density**. Aluminum has the highest charge density because it has the smallest radius and the highest ionic charge ($3+$).

steps.title: Link to melting point
steps.content:
The high charge density of $\text{Al}^{3+}$ creates a much stronger electrostatic attraction to the delocalized electrons compared to $\text{Na}^+$. 

Because the metallic bonds in aluminum are stronger, more thermal energy is required to overcome these attractions and melt the metal. This results in the observed melting point order:

$$\text{Al (highest)} > \text{Mg} > \text{Na (lowest)}$$

highlights.text: resulting in stronger attraction and a higher melting point

steps.title: Analyze electron delocalization
steps.content:
In metallic bonding, the melting point is determined by the strength of the electrostatic attraction between the metal cations and the sea of delocalized electrons. Transition metals, such as Ti and Fe, have partially filled $d$-orbitals. Unlike aluminum (which only delocalizes its $3s$ and $3p$ valence electrons), transition metals can delocalize electrons from both their $ns$ and $(n-1)d$ sublevels into the metallic lattice.

This concept is explored in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**, specifically focusing on how $d$-electrons contribute to bond strength in transition elements.

highlights.text: partially filled d-orbitals

steps.title: Bond strength and packing
steps.content:
Because transition metals contribute more delocalized electrons to the sea, the resulting electrostatic attraction (the metallic bond) is significantly stronger than in aluminum. Furthermore, transition metals typically have a smaller atomic radius and more efficient, denser atomic packing in their crystal lattice. 

Combining these factors—**stronger metallic bonding** and **denser packing**—results in a much higher energy requirement to break the lattice, explaining why Ti and Fe have higher melting points than Al.

highlights.text: higher melting points than Al

steps.title: Final Summary for Marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must address both the electronic and structural reasons:

1.  **Electronic**: Transition metals have partially filled $d$-orbitals that contribute more delocalized electrons to the sea of electrons.
2.  **Structural**: This leads to stronger metallic bonds and often results in a denser atomic packing compared to Al.

steps.title: Define density qualitatively
steps.content:
To understand why iron (Fe) is denser than sodium (Na), we need to look at the formula for density:

$$\text{Density} = \frac{\text{Mass}}{\text{Volume}}$$

In chemistry, the density of a metal is determined by the mass of its individual atoms (atomic mass) and how many of those atoms can fit into a specific space (packing).

steps.title: Compare atomic masses
steps.content:
The first factor is the mass of the particles themselves. If you look at the periodic table, you'll see that iron (Fe) has a much higher relative atomic mass than sodium (Na).

- Fe: $\approx 55.85\text{ u}$
- Na: $\approx 22.99\text{ u}$

This means each individual iron atom is more than twice as heavy as a sodium atom.

highlights.text: Fe has a greater atomic mass than Na

steps.title: Analyze packing and radius
steps.content:
The second factor is the volume occupied by the atoms. Iron atoms have a smaller atomic radius than sodium atoms. Because the atoms are smaller, they can be packed more closely together in the metallic lattice. 

Tighter packing means you can fit more atoms into the same amount of space compared to a metal with larger atoms like sodium.

highlights.text: Smaller radius in Fe allows tighter packing

steps.title: Relate to density
steps.content: When we combine these two factors—iron atoms are heavier AND they pack more tightly—the result is that iron has a significantly higher mass per unit volume. This explains why iron is much denser than sodium.

highlights.text: More mass per unit volume
