steps.title: Identify iron's delocalized electrons
steps.content:
Iron ($Fe$) is a transition metal. Unlike main-group metals, transition metals can delocalize a higher number of electrons because they use both their $4s$ and $3d$ electrons in bonding. 

Having a high number of delocalized electrons significantly increases the "glue" holding the metal together.

highlights.text: iron

steps.title: Analyze attraction strength
steps.content:
Because iron provides more delocalized electrons to the "sea," there is a much **stronger electrostatic attraction** between these electrons and the $Fe$ cations. 

This increased attraction pulls the lattice together more tightly and increases the overall bond strength.

steps.title: Link to hardness
steps.content:
Hardness is a measure of a material's **resistance to deformation**. In a metal, deformation occurs when layers of ions slide over each other. 

Because the electrostatic attraction in iron is so strong, it is much harder for these layers to move, resulting in the greater hardness mentioned in the question.

highlights.text: greater hardness of iron

steps.title: Define metallic bonding
steps.content:
To understand hardness, we first look at the metallic model. Metallic bonding is the electrostatic attraction between a regular lattice of positive metal cations and a "sea" of delocalized valence electrons. 

This concept is covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**. The strength of this attraction determines the physical properties of the metal, such as its melting point and hardness.

steps.title: Compare delocalized electrons
steps.content:
Hardness increases when there are more delocalized electrons. 

Comparing iron ($Fe$) to s-block metals like sodium ($Na$) or magnesium ($Mg$):
- Sodium has only 1 delocalized electron per atom ($3s^1$).
- Iron, a transition metal, can delocalize both its $4s$ and several $3d$ electrons.

Because iron provides a higher number of delocalized electrons to the "sea," the resulting electrostatic attraction is much stronger. This aligns with **Topic S2.3.3**, which notes that transition elements use d-electrons to strengthen metallic bonds.

highlights.text: Stronger electrostatic attraction in Fe due to more delocalized electrons

steps.title: Analyze cation charge
steps.content:
In addition to more electrons, iron forms cations with a higher charge (typically $Fe^{2+}$ or $Fe^{3+}$) compared to s-block metals like $Na^+$. 

According to Coulomb's Law, a higher charge on the cation results in a stronger attraction to the delocalized electrons. This further increases the overall bond strength within the iron lattice.

steps.title: Link to resistance to deformation
steps.content:
Hardness is defined by how well a material resists being permanently deformed or scratched. 

Because the metallic bonds in iron are so strong, it requires significantly more force to make the layers of cations slide over one another. This **greater bond strength increases the resistance to deformation**, which we observe as greater hardness compared to the much softer s-block metals.

highlights.text: Greater bond strength increases resistance to deformation

steps.title: Define Metallic Bonding
steps.content:
To understand hardness, we first need to look at what holds a metal together. Metallic bonding is the electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions (cations) and a "sea" of delocalized electrons. 

In iron ($Fe$), there is a particularly strong attraction because iron can delocalize more electrons (including some from its 3d subshell) compared to metals in Group 1 or 2. This concept is covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**.

steps.title: Identify Bond Strength
steps.content:
The strength of a metallic bond depends on the charge of the metal ion and the number of delocalized electrons. Because iron has a higher number of delocalized electrons, the electrostatic attraction between the ions and the electron sea is very intense. 

This strong attraction acts like a powerful "glue," holding the atomic structure firmly together. This satisfies the first mark from the markscheme.

highlights.text: Stronger electrostatic attraction in Fe due to more delocalized electrons

steps.title: Explain Resistance to Deformation
steps.content:
Hardness is physically defined as a material's resistance to permanent shape change (deformation). In a metal, deformation occurs when layers of metal ions slide over one another when a force is applied.

Because the metallic bonds in iron are so strong, they provide significant resistance to this movement. The powerful electrostatic forces prevent the layers from easily shifting out of their positions.

steps.title: Connect Bonding to Hardness
steps.content:
Finally, we link the bond strength to the physical property. Since the strong metallic bonds make it very difficult for the layers of iron ions to slide past each other, the metal does not dent or scratch easily. 

This high resistance to the sliding of layers is exactly why iron is classified as a hard metal. This fulfills the second mark point regarding resistance to deformation.

highlights.text: Greater bond strength increases resistance to deformation

steps.title: Define the lattice structure
steps.content:
In a metallic substance, the metal atoms lose their outer shell electrons to achieve stability, becoming positive ions known as **cations**. These cations are not arranged randomly; they are organized in a fixed, repeating three-dimensional pattern called a **regular lattice**.

This lattice structure is a fundamental part of metallic bonding and provides the physical framework for the metal.

steps.title: Identify the electron sea
steps.content:
The valence electrons that were lost by the metal atoms do not stay with their original nuclei. Instead, they are free to move throughout the entire structure. These are described as **delocalized electrons**.

They form a **'sea' of delocalized electrons** that surrounds and permeates the lattice of positive ions, acting as the 'glue' that holds the structure together through electrostatic attraction.

steps.title: Summarize for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks in an IB exam for this description, you should focus on these two specific components:

1.  **Regular lattice of positive ions (cations)**: This identifies the stationary part of the structure.
2.  **Sea of delocalized electrons**: This identifies the mobile part of the structure.

This model explains why metals are good conductors of heat and electricity, as the delocalized electrons can transfer energy easily through the lattice.

parts.content: Explain how this structure enables thermal conductivity.
parts.markscheme:
- Explain that delocalized electrons move and transfer kinetic energy through collisions with other particles <marks>A1</marks>
- State that thermal energy is also transferred through the lattice via the vibration of ions being passed to neighboring ions <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Recall metallic structure
steps.content:
To explain thermal conductivity, we first need to recall the structure of a metal. As covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**, metals consist of a regular lattice of positive metal ions (cations) surrounded by a "sea" of delocalized electrons. 

Thermal energy can be transferred through this structure in two distinct ways: through the movement of electrons and through the vibration of the ions themselves.

steps.title: Movement of delocalized electrons
steps.content:
The first and most efficient mechanism involves the delocalized electrons. Because these electrons are not bound to any specific atom, they are free to move throughout the entire lattice.

When a metal is heated, the delocalized electrons at the hot end gain **kinetic energy**. They move rapidly toward the cooler regions of the metal, where they collide with other electrons and metal ions, transferring their energy and spreading the heat quickly.

highlights.text: delocalized electrons move and transfer kinetic energy through collisions with other particles

steps.title: Vibration of lattice ions
steps.content:
The second mechanism occurs within the lattice of positive ions. Although the ions are in fixed positions, they are constantly vibrating. 

As the temperature increases, the ions at the heat source vibrate more vigorously (with greater amplitude). Because the ions are closely packed and held together by metallic bonds, these vibrations are passed along to neighboring ions, effectively conducting thermal energy through the material.

highlights.text: thermal energy is also transferred through the lattice via the vibration of ions being passed to neighboring ions

steps.title: Summary for marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must mention both components of the "dual mechanism":
1.  **Electrons:** Delocalized electrons move and transfer kinetic energy via collisions. (1 mark)
2.  **Lattice:** Thermal energy is transferred as vibrations are passed between neighboring ions. (1 mark)

1 mark total

steps.title: Recall metallic properties
steps.content: This question relates to **Topic S2.3.1: Metallic bonding and properties of metals**. One of the defining physical properties of metals is their high thermal conductivity. This occurs because the delocalized electrons in the metallic lattice can move freely, carrying kinetic energy rapidly through the material.

highlights.text: Metals can conduct heat efficiently

steps.title: Identify heat addition needs
steps.content: In practical applications, we often need to transfer thermal energy from a heat source (like a gas flame or electric element) to a substance (like food or water). To do this effectively, the material separating the source and the substance must be an excellent conductor to ensure the heat is added quickly and evenly.

steps.title: Suggest a practical use
steps.content: A common practical use is in **cooking utensils**, such as **saucepans** or **frying pans**. Metals like copper, aluminum, or stainless steel are used for the base of these pans so that heat from the stove is efficiently transferred to the food inside.

steps.title: Final answer and markscheme
steps.content:
According to the markscheme, any application that involves efficient heat transfer is acceptable. 

Examples include:
- **Saucepans** (for cooking)
- **Radiators** (for heating rooms)
- **Heat sinks** (for cooling electronic components like computer CPUs)
- **Engine parts** (to dissipate heat from combustion)

Choosing **saucepans** is a clear and simple example of a heat addition application.

highlights.text: Suggest a practical application such as saucepans, radiators, heat sinks, or engine parts

steps.title: Identify the property
steps.content: The question notes that metals conduct heat efficiently. This high thermal conductivity is a result of **metallic bonding**, where delocalized electrons are free to move and transfer kinetic energy quickly throughout the metal lattice. This is covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**.

highlights.text: Metals can conduct heat efficiently.

steps.title: Understanding heat dissipation
steps.content: In many practical scenarios, heat is a byproduct that must be removed from a system to prevent it from failing or melting. This process is called **heat dissipation**. Because metals transfer heat so well, they are the ideal material for components designed to 'wick' heat away from a source and release it into the environment.

steps.title: Select a practical application
steps.content:
Using the **Heat Dissipation Applications** approach, we can identify several specific examples:

*   **Computer Heat Sinks**: Often made of aluminum or copper, these sit on top of a CPU to pull heat away from the sensitive electronic chips.
*   **Car Radiators**: These use metal fins to transfer heat from the engine's coolant to the outside air, preventing the engine from overheating.
*   **Engine Cooling Fins**: Found on motorcycle or lawnmower engines to increase surface area for heat to escape.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you just need to suggest one of these applications. According to the markscheme, any of the following are acceptable:

*   **Car radiators**
*   **Computer heat sinks**
*   **Engine parts**
*   **Saucepans** (while these use heat for cooking, they still rely on the same conductivity property!)

parts.content: Write the electron configuration of $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}$.

steps.title: Identify neutral Copper's electrons
steps.content: To find the configuration of the $\text{Cu}^{+}$ ion, we first look at the neutral copper atom. From the periodic table, copper has an atomic number of 29, meaning it has 29 electrons.

highlights.text: 29 electrons

steps.title: Apply the copper exception
steps.content:
According to the Aufbau principle, we might expect the configuration to end in $4s^2 3d^9$. However, copper is a well-known exception in the IB syllabus. To achieve a more stable, full $3d$ sublevel, one electron promotes from the $4s$ to the $3d$ orbital.

Neutral Cu configuration:
$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^1 3d^{10}$$

highlights.text: 4s^1 3d^{10} exception

steps.title: Remove the $4s$ electron
steps.content:
When transition metals form ions, a critical rule to remember is that electrons are always removed from the **$4s$ orbital before the $3d$ orbital**. 

To form the $\text{Cu}^{+}$ ion, we need to remove one electron from the neutral atom configuration we just wrote.

steps.title: Determine final configuration
steps.content:
Removing the single electron from the $4s^1$ subshell leaves us with a full $3d^{10}$ subshell. 

$$\text{Cu}^{+} \text{ configuration: } 1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^{10}$$

highlights.text: Write the electron configuration of \mathrm{Cu}^{+}

steps.title: Determine number of electrons
steps.content:
To find the electron configuration of the ion, we first look at the atomic number of Copper ($Z = 29$). 

The $\text{Cu}^{+}$ ion has a positive charge, meaning it has lost one electron. We calculate the total number of electrons remaining:

$$29 - 1 = 28 \text{ electrons}$$

highlights.text: electron configuration of $\mathrm{Cu}^{+}$

steps.title: Apply transition metal rule
steps.content:
For transition metal ions, there is a specific rule: electrons are always removed from the $4s$ subshell before the $3d$ subshell. 

Because we are forming a $\text{Cu}^{+}$ ion, the $4s$ subshell will be empty ($4s^0$). This is a key concept in **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations** regarding how d-block elements behave.

steps.title: Fill the orbitals
steps.content:
Now, we distribute the 28 electrons into the subshells in order of increasing energy, ensuring the $4s$ orbital is left empty:

1.  $1s^2$ (2 electrons)
2.  $2s^2$ (4 total)
3.  $2p^6$ (10 total)
4.  $3s^2$ (12 total)
5.  $3p^6$ (18 total)
6.  $3d^{10}$ (28 total)

Note that the $3d$ subshell is filled to its maximum capacity of 10 electrons to reach the total count of 28.

steps.title: State the final configuration
steps.content:
The full electron configuration for the $\text{Cu}^{+}$ ion is:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^{10}$$

(Note: You do not need to include $4s^0$ in your final answer unless specifically asked).

parts.content: Explain why there is a relatively large difference between the first and second ionization energies of copper.
parts.markscheme:
- The second ionization energy removes electron from stable/closed ${3d^{10}}$ configuration, requiring much more energy <marks>1 mark</marks>

- The first ionization energy removes electron from 4s subshell where electron is relatively shielded <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the goal
steps.content: To explain the large jump in energy between the first and second ionization energies (IE), we need to look at which subshells the electrons are being removed from. This concept is covered in **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**.

highlights.text: relatively large difference between the first and second ionization energies

steps.title: Write Copper's electron configuration
steps.content:
First, let's write the electron configuration for a neutral copper atom ($Z = 29$). Copper is a well-known exception to the Aufbau principle because a full $3d$ subshell is particularly stable:

$$\text{Cu: } [Ar] \, 3d^{10} 4s^1$$

When atoms form ions, the electrons in the highest main energy level (the $4s$ orbital) are removed first.

steps.title: Analyze the first ionization
steps.content:
The **first ionization energy** ($IE_1$) corresponds to removing the single electron from the $4s$ subshell:

$$\text{Cu}(g) \rightarrow \text{Cu}^+(g) + e^- \quad \text{Config of Cu}^+: [Ar] \, 3d^{10}$$

This electron is relatively far from the nucleus and is shielded by the inner $3d$ electrons, so it requires a comparatively low amount of energy to remove.

steps.title: Analyze the second ionization
steps.content:
The **second ionization energy** ($IE_2$) involves removing an electron from the $\text{Cu}^+$ ion:

$$\text{Cu}^+(g) \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+}(g) + e^- \quad \text{Config of Cu}^{2+}: [Ar] \, 3d^9$$

This electron must be removed from a **stable, full $3d^{10}$ subshell**. Because this subshell is closer to the nucleus and is already "closed" (full), it is much more difficult to remove an electron, leading to a much higher energy requirement.

steps.title: Final Conclusion
steps.content:
To summarize for your exam marks:
1. The first electron is removed from the **$4s$ subshell**, which is shielded and further from the nucleus.
2. The second electron is removed from a **stable $3d^{10}$ configuration**, which requires significantly more energy to break.

steps.title: Identify the electron configuration
steps.content:
To understand ionization energy, we first need to look at the electron configuration of a neutral copper atom ($Cu$). 

Copper is an exception to the general filling rules. Instead of $[Ar] 4s^2 3d^9$, it has a full $d$-subshell for extra stability:

$$[Ar] 3d^{10} 4s^1$$

This tells us that the first electron removed will come from the $4s$ orbital, and the second will come from the $3d$ orbital.

steps.title: Analyze the first ionization
steps.content:
The **first ionization energy** involves removing the single electron from the $4s$ subshell. 

In terms of **shielding and energy levels**:
- The $4s$ electron is in the $n=4$ main energy level, which is the furthest from the nucleus.
- It is highly shielded from the positive pull of the nucleus by the 18 electrons in the inner shells ($n=1, 2, \text{ and } 3$).

Because it is far away and well-shielded, it requires relatively little energy to remove.

highlights.text: The first ionization energy removes electron from 4s subshell where electron is relatively shielded

steps.title: Analyze the second ionization
steps.content:
The **second ionization energy** involves removing an electron from the $Cu^+$ ion, which has the configuration $[Ar] 3d^{10}$.

Now, the electron must be removed from the $3d$ subshell ($n=3$ level):
- The $n=3$ level is closer to the nucleus than the $n=4$ level.
- Once the $4s$ electron is gone, the remaining electrons experience a significantly higher **effective nuclear charge** because there is less shielding from outer electrons.
- Furthermore, the $3d^{10}$ configuration is a **stable, closed subshell**.

highlights.text: The second ionization energy removes electron from stable/closed 3d^{10} configuration, requiring much more energy

steps.title: Conclude the energy difference
steps.content:
The large difference arises because we are moving from removing a weakly-held, highly-shielded electron in a higher energy level ($n=4$) to removing an electron from a lower, more stable energy level ($n=3$) that is closer to the nucleus and less shielded.

This concept is covered in **Topic S1.3.7: Successive ionization energies**. Understanding how $n$ levels and shielding affect the pull of the nucleus is key to predicting these jumps!

parts.content: Calculate the oxidation state of copper in $\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}$.
parts.markscheme:
- Let oxidation state of O = -2 <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Total charge must equal 0: $2x + (-2) = 0$ where x is oxidation state of Cu

- Therefore oxidation state of Cu = +1

<note>Award full marks for correct final answer of +1 with or without working</note>

steps.title: Identify oxygen's oxidation state
steps.content:
To find the oxidation state of copper, we start with the known rules. In most compounds, oxygen has an oxidation state of $-2$. This is a fundamental concept found in **Topic 3.1: The periodic table: Classification of elements** (S3.1.6).

Let the oxidation state of oxygen be $-2$.

highlights.text: $\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}$

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
A key rule for neutral compounds is that the sum of the oxidation states of all atoms must equal $0$. 

Let $x$ represent the oxidation state of copper. Since there are two copper atoms and one oxygen atom in $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$, we can set up the following algebraic equation:

$$2(x) + 1(-2) = 0$$

highlights.text: oxidation state of copper

steps.title: Solve for copper
steps.content:
Now, we solve for $x$ to find the oxidation state of a single copper atom:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x - 2 &= 0 \\ 2x &= 2 \\ x &= +1 \end{aligned}$$

In oxidation states, it is important to always include the sign (even for positive values).

gdc.instructions: Use the equation solver mode on your calculator. Input the equation 2x - 2 = 0 and solve for x.

ti84.instructions: Go to the 'Numeric Solver' by pressing [MATH] and then [0] (Solver...). Enter the equation: 0 = 2x - 2 and press [OK] (Graph), then [SOLVE] (Graph).

desmos.instructions: Plot the line y = 2x - 2 and find where it intersects the x-axis (where y = 0).

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content:
The oxidation state of copper in $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$ is $+1$. 

This makes sense as copper is a transition metal that can exhibit multiple oxidation states, and in this specific oxide, it exists as the $\text{Cu}^{+}$ ion.

steps.title: Identify the Oxide Ion
steps.content:
To find the oxidation state using the ionic charge balancing method, we first identify the charge of the known ion. Oxygen almost always forms the oxide ion, $O^{2-}$, when combined with metals. This is because oxygen belongs to Group 16 and gains two electrons to reach a stable configuration.

This concept is a key part of **Topic S3.1.6: Oxidation states**.

highlights.text: Oxidation states

steps.title: Set Up Charge Balance
steps.content:
A neutral compound like $\mathrm{Cu}_2\mathrm{O}$ has a total net charge of zero. Based on the chemical formula, we have two copper ions for every one oxide ion. We can represent the unknown oxidation state of copper as $x$.

According to the principle of electrical neutrality:

$$\text{Total Charge} = (2 \times \text{charge of Cu}) + (1 \times \text{charge of O}) = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for Copper's Charge
steps.content:
Now, we substitute the charge of the oxide ion ($-2$) into our equation and solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x + (-2) &= 0 \\ 2x &= +2 \\ x &= +1 \end{aligned}$$

Each copper ion must have a charge of $+1$ to balance the $-2$ charge from the single oxygen atom.

calculator.description: Calculate the charge per copper atom by dividing the total positive charge required by the number of copper atoms.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The oxidation state of copper in $\mathrm{Cu}_2\mathrm{O}$ is **+1**.

In the IB markscheme, you earn the mark by identifying that the sum of charges must be zero and correctly calculating the value. Note that oxidation states must always be written with the sign first (e.g., $+1$ rather than $1+$).

highlights.text: Therefore oxidation state of Cu = +1

steps.title: Identify the IUPAC name
steps.content: To find the oxidation state using the nomenclature approach, we look directly at the name of the compound provided in the question: **Copper(I) oxide**.

highlights.text: Copper(I) oxide

steps.title: Interpret the Roman numeral
steps.content:
In the IUPAC naming system, transition metals (like copper) can have multiple possible oxidation states. To specify which one is present in a compound, a Roman numeral is written in parentheses immediately after the metal's name. 

This numeral indicates the **oxidation state** of the metal ion.

steps.title: Determine the value
steps.content:
The name contains the Roman numeral **(I)**. In chemistry notation, this corresponds to a value of $+1$. 

This is a quick and effective way to identify oxidation states without needing to perform a full algebraic calculation using the chemical formula $\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}$. This concept is covered in **Topic S3.1.6: Oxidation states**.

highlights.text: Copper(I) oxide

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Therefore, the oxidation state of copper in $\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}$ is $+1$. 

As noted in the markscheme, you receive full credit for stating the correct oxidation state, whether or not you show the algebraic working.

parts.content: Describe the type of bonding present in $\mathrm{Cu}_2 \mathrm{O}$, and explain how this type of bonding affects its physical properties.
parts.markscheme:
- $\mathrm{Cu}_2\mathrm{O}$ exhibits ionic bonding between $\mathrm{Cu}^+$ and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions <marks>1 mark</marks>

- Ionic bonding results in high melting/boiling points and makes it a good electrical insulator in solid state (as ions are fixed in place) <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the bonding type
steps.content:
To determine the bonding in $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$, we look at the elements involved. Copper ($\text{Cu}$) is a metal and Oxygen ($\text{O}$) is a non-metal. 

When a metal reacts with a non-metal, electrons are transferred to form ions. In this compound, we have $\text{Cu}^+$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ ions. The bonding is **ionic**, which is defined as the electrostatic attraction between oppositely charged ions.

steps.title: Explain high melting point
steps.content: In the **Standard Ionic Model**, these ions are arranged in a regular 3D crystal lattice. Because the electrostatic forces of attraction between the $\text{Cu}^+$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ ions are very strong, a large amount of thermal energy is required to overcome these bonds. This results in $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$ having a **high melting point**.

steps.title: Analyze electrical conductivity
steps.content:
For a substance to conduct electricity, it must have mobile charged particles (like electrons or ions). 

In the solid state, the ions in $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$ are **fixed in place** within the lattice and cannot move to carry a charge. Therefore, solid $\text{Cu}_2\text{O}$ acts as an **electrical insulator**.

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
To earn full marks on this question, you must mention both the specific ions and the link between the structure and properties:

1.  **1 mark**: Identifying the bonding as ionic between $\text{Cu}^+$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ ions.
2.  **1 mark**: Explaining that this leads to a high melting point OR that it is an insulator in the solid state because ions are fixed.

parts.content: State the full electronic configuration of $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$.

steps.title: Identify Cobalt's atomic number
steps.content:
To find the electronic configuration, we first need to know how many electrons a neutral Cobalt atom has. Looking at the Periodic Table in your data booklet, Cobalt is element number 27.

$$Z = 27$$ 

This means a neutral Cobalt atom has 27 protons and 27 electrons.

highlights.text: cobalt in a +2 oxidation state

steps.title: Write the neutral configuration
steps.content:
Following the Aufbau principle (covered in **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**), we fill orbitals in order of increasing energy: $1s \rightarrow 2s \rightarrow 2p \rightarrow 3s \rightarrow 3p \rightarrow 4s \rightarrow 3d$.

For 27 electrons, the configuration is:
$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^2 3d^7$$

steps.title: Remove valence electrons
steps.content:
The question asks for the configuration of $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$. A $+2$ charge means the atom has lost two electrons. 

A critical rule for transition metals is that they **lose electrons from the $4s$ orbital before the $3d$ orbital**, even though the $3d$ was filled last. 

Removing the two electrons from the $4s^2$ subshell leaves us with:
$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^7$$

highlights.text: full electronic configuration of \mathrm{Co}^{2+}

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The full electronic configuration for $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$ is:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^7$$

In an IB exam, you'll get 1 mark for providing this exact sequence correctly. Note that it is standard to write the $3d$ subshell before or after the $4s$ in the neutral atom, but for the ion, the $4s$ is entirely empty and should not be included in the "full" configuration unless specified.

steps.title: Locate Cobalt
steps.content:
First, we look at the periodic table to find Cobalt ($\text{Co}$). It is located in the first row of the d-block with an atomic number of 27.

This means a neutral cobalt atom has **27 electrons**.

steps.title: Account for the charge
steps.content:
The question specifies cobalt is in a **+2 oxidation state**, meaning we are looking for the configuration of the $\text{Co}^{2+}$ ion. A $+2$ charge means the atom has lost 2 electrons.

$$\text{Total electrons in } \text{Co}^{2+} = 27 - 2 = 25$$

gdc.instructions: Subtract the charge (2) from the atomic number (27).

ti84.instructions: Subtract the charge (2) from the atomic number (27).

desmos.instructions: Subtract the charge (2) from the atomic number (27).

calculator.description: Calculate the number of electrons for the Co2+ ion.

highlights.text: +2 oxidation state

steps.title: Apply transition metal rules
steps.content:
When transition metals in the first d-series (Sc to Zn) form ions, they **lose their $4s$ electrons first**. 

Since we are forming a $+2$ ion, the two $4s$ electrons that would be present in a neutral cobalt atom ($4s^2$) are completely removed. Therefore, the $\text{Co}^{2+}$ ion will have **zero electrons in the $4s$ orbital**.

steps.title: Fill subshells directly
steps.content:
Now we fill the subshells in order until we reach the 3p level, which uses 18 electrons (matching the Argon core):

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6$$

To find how many electrons are left for the $3d$ subshell, we subtract these 18 electrons from our total of 25:

$$25 - 18 = 7 \text{ electrons}$$

We place these remaining 7 electrons directly into the $3d$ subshell to get $3d^7$.

ti84.instructions: Subtract 18 (the Argon core) from the 25 electrons in the ion.

calculator.description: Determine the remaining electrons for the 3d subshell after filling up to 3p.

steps.title: State final configuration
steps.content:
Combining everything together, we list the subshells in order of increasing energy levels. Note that since the $4s$ subshell is empty, it is omitted from the full configuration.

The full electronic configuration is:
$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^7$$

parts.content: Explain why the atomic radius of cobalt is smaller than that of calcium, despite both being in the same period.
parts.markscheme:
- Cobalt has more protons (higher nuclear charge) than calcium <marks>[1]</marks>

- The increased nuclear charge attracts the electron cloud more strongly, pulling the electrons closer to the nucleus and resulting in a smaller atomic radius <marks>[1]</marks>

steps.title: Compare nuclear charges
steps.content:
First, we look at the atomic numbers of both elements on the periodic table. Calcium (Ca) has an atomic number of 20, while Cobalt (Co) has an atomic number of 27. 

Because the atomic number represents the number of protons in the nucleus, cobalt has a significantly higher **nuclear charge** than calcium.

$$\text{Protons in Co} = 27$$
$$\text{Protons in Ca} = 20$$

highlights.text: Cobalt has more protons (higher nuclear charge) than calcium

steps.title: Evaluate shielding and attraction
steps.content:
Both cobalt and calcium are in **Period 4**, which means their valence electrons are being added to the same main energy level ($n=4$). 

Because they are in the same period, the shielding effect from inner-shell electrons remains relatively constant. With shielding held constant, the extra protons in cobalt exert a much stronger electrostatic attraction on the outer electron cloud.

steps.title: Relate attraction to radius
steps.content: The increased nuclear charge in cobalt attracts the electron cloud more strongly. This stronger pull draws the electrons closer to the nucleus, which results in a smaller atomic radius compared to calcium.

highlights.text: The increased nuclear charge attracts the electron cloud more strongly, pulling the electrons closer to the nucleus and resulting in a smaller atomic radius

parts.content: Calculate the oxidation state of oxygen in CoO.
parts.markscheme:
- Oxidation state of oxygen = $-2$ <marks>[1]</marks>

<note>Accept -II or negative two</note>

steps.title: Identify the sum rule
steps.content:
To solve this using the **Sum of Oxidation States** approach, we use the rule that the sum of the oxidation states of all atoms in a neutral compound must equal zero. This concept is a core part of **Topic R3.2: Electron transfer reactions**.

We are given that cobalt is in a +2 oxidation state.

highlights.text: cobalt in a +2 oxidation state

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
Let $x$ be the oxidation state of oxygen. Since the formula for cobalt(II) oxide is $\text{CoO}$, there is one atom of cobalt and one atom of oxygen. We can set up the following algebraic equation:

$$(\text{number of Co} \times \text{ox. state of Co}) + (\text{number of O} \times \text{ox. state of O}) = 0$$

Substituting the known values:
$$(1 \times (+2)) + (1 \times x) = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for oxygen
steps.content:
Now, we just need to solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} 2 + x &= 0 \\ x &= -2 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Simple subtraction to solve the linear equation.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The oxidation state of oxygen in $\text{CoO}$ is $-2$. 

In IB Chemistry, remember that oxidation states are always written with the sign (+ or -) **before** the number.

steps.title: Identify cobalt ion charge
steps.content: From the question specification, we are told that cobalt is in a **+2 oxidation state**. In the context of an ionic compound like cobalt(II) oxide, this means the cobalt exists as a $Co^{2+}$ ion within the lattice.

highlights.text: cobalt in a +2 oxidation state

steps.title: Analyze the formula ratio
steps.content:
The chemical formula provided is $CoO$. This indicates a **1:1 ratio** between cobalt ions and oxygen ions in the crystal lattice. 

This topic is part of **Structure 2.1: The ionic model**, which explores how binary compounds are formed from ions.

highlights.text: CoO

steps.title: Apply electrical neutrality
steps.content:
For any ionic compound to be stable, it must be electrically neutral, meaning the sum of all positive and negative charges must equal zero. 

Since we have one $Co^{2+}$ ion, the single oxygen ion must have a charge that cancels out the $+2$ charge:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Total Charge} &= (\text{Charge of Co}) + (\text{Charge of O}) \\ 0 &= (+2) + x \\ x &= -2 \end{aligned}$$

This means the oxygen exists as an oxide ion, $O^{2-}$.

steps.title: State the oxidation state
steps.content:
In simple binary ionic compounds, the oxidation state of an element is equal to the charge of its ion. Since the oxygen ion has a charge of $2-$, its oxidation state is **$-2$**.

According to the markscheme, you can write this as $-2$, -II, or negative two to earn the mark.

steps.title: Identify given information
steps.content:
The question specifies that we are looking at **Cobalt(II) oxide (CoO)** and explicitly states that cobalt has an oxidation state of $+2$. 

This is a great starting point because the Roman numeral in the name "Cobalt(II)" directly tells us the oxidation state of the metal ion.

highlights.text: cobalt in a +2 oxidation state

steps.title: Apply oxygen's oxidation rule
steps.content:
According to the standard oxidation rules (covered in **Topic S3.1.6: Oxidation states**), oxygen is assigned an oxidation state of $-2$ in most of its compounds, such as oxides.

The only common exceptions to this rule are:
1. **Peroxides** (like $H_2O_2$), where oxygen is $-1$.
2. **Compounds with fluorine** (like $OF_2$), where oxygen can be positive.

Since CoO is a simple metal oxide, we expect oxygen to follow the general rule.

steps.title: Verify using sum rule
steps.content:
In a neutral compound like CoO, the sum of the oxidation states of all atoms must equal zero. We can set up a simple equation to verify our answer:

$$\begin{aligned} (\text{Oxidation state of Co}) + (\text{Oxidation state of O}) &= 0 \\ (+2) + (x) &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

Solving for $x$:
$$x = -2$$

This confirms that the oxidation state of oxygen is $-2$, which is consistent with the $+2$ state of cobalt.

ti84.instructions: Enter the sum of the known cobalt state and the calculated oxygen state on the home screen.

desmos.instructions: Evaluate the sum in the expression bar.

calculator.description: Verify the sum of oxidation states is zero.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The oxidation state of oxygen in CoO is **$-2$**.

Note that in Chemistry, we write the sign before the number (e.g., $-2$ or $-II$) for oxidation states, whereas for ionic charges, we typically write the number first (e.g., $2-$).

parts.content: Describe the bonding in cobalt metal, $\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{s})$, and explain how it differs from the bonding in CoO.
parts.markscheme:
- Metallic bonding in $\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{s})$: lattice of positive ions surrounded by sea of delocalized electrons <marks>[1]</marks>

- Ionic bonding in CoO: $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$ and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions held together by electrostatic attraction <marks>[1]</marks>

<note>Accept clear descriptions of the bonding types and differences between them</note>

steps.title: Describe metallic bonding in Co
steps.content:
To understand the bonding in cobalt metal, $\mathrm{Co}(\mathrm{s})$, we look at its structure as an element. In a metallic lattice, the atoms lose their outer electrons to form a "sea" of mobile electrons. 

Cobalt consists of a **lattice of positive ions (cations)** surrounded by **delocalized electrons**. The bond itself is the strong **electrostatic attraction** between these positive ions and the sea of delocalized electrons.

highlights.text: lattice of positive ions surrounded by sea of delocalized electrons

steps.title: Describe ionic bonding in CoO
steps.content:
In contrast, cobalt(II) oxide ($\mathrm{CoO}$) is a compound formed between a metal and a non-metal. This results in **ionic bonding**. 

In $\mathrm{CoO}$, cobalt exists as $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$ cations and oxygen exists as $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ anions. These oppositely charged ions are held together in a 3D lattice by strong **electrostatic attractions** between the **cations and anions**.

highlights.text: Co2+ and O2- ions held together by electrostatic attraction

steps.title: Compare the bonding types
steps.content:
The fundamental difference lies in what the positive cobalt ions are attracted to:

- In **metallic cobalt**, the attraction is between the $\mathrm{Co}$ cations and **delocalized electrons**.
- In **cobalt(II) oxide**, the attraction is between the $\mathrm{Co}^{2+}$ cations and **$\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ anions**.

This distinction is covered in **Topic S2: Models of bonding and structure** in your syllabus.

parts.content: Describe the trend in atomic radius from Sc to Ni and explain how this trend relates to the strength of metallic bonding.
parts.markscheme:
- Atomic radius decreases from Sc to Ni <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Due to increasing nuclear charge pulling electrons closer to the nucleus (with similar shielding) <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Smaller atomic radius results in stronger metallic bonding because the delocalized electrons are closer to the cations, increasing electrostatic attraction <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Award full marks only if both trend and explanation are clearly linked to metallic bonding strength</note>

steps.title: Identify the trend
steps.content:
By looking at the provided data table, we can see that the atomic radius values for Sc, Fe, and Ni are $162\text{ pm}$, $126\text{ pm}$, and $124\text{ pm}$ respectively. 

This shows a clear trend: the **atomic radius decreases** as we move from Sc to Ni across the period.

steps.title: Explain radius decrease
steps.content:
This decrease occurs because, from Sc to Ni, the number of protons in the nucleus increases (**increasing nuclear charge**). While electrons are added to the $3d$ subshell, the **shielding effect** remains relatively constant because these electrons are in the same inner shell. 

Consequently, the **effective nuclear charge** ($Z_{eff}$) increases, which pulls the outer electrons closer to the nucleus, reducing the atomic radius. This concept is covered in **Topic 3: Periodicity** (S3.1.3).

steps.title: Link to bonding strength
steps.content:
Metallic bonding is defined as the electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions and a "sea" of delocalized electrons. 

A smaller atomic radius means that the delocalized electrons are **closer to the nuclei** of the metal cations. According to Coulomb's Law, the force of attraction is inversely proportional to the square of the distance ($r$):

$$F ∝ \frac{q_1 q_2}{r^2}$$

Since $r$ is smaller for Ni than for Sc, the electrostatic attraction is stronger, leading to **stronger metallic bonding**.

parts.content: Explain the higher density of copper (Cu) compared to iron (Fe), referencing the data provided.
parts.markscheme:
- Density depends on atomic mass and atomic radius/volume <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Copper has a higher atomic mass than Iron which outweighs the slight difference in atomic radius/volume <marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Define density conceptually
steps.content:
Density is fundamentally the ratio of mass to volume. For metallic elements, we can think of this as the ratio of the mass of an atom to the volume it occupies in the crystal lattice:

$$\text{Density} \approx \frac{\text{Atomic Mass}}{\text{Atomic Volume}}$$

Atomic volume is directly related to the atomic radius ($V \propto r^3$), so changes in either mass or radius will affect the density.

steps.title: Analyze the volume factor
steps.content:
According to the provided data, the atomic radius of Iron (Fe) is **126 pm** and the atomic radius of Copper (Cu) is **128 pm**. 

Because these radii are very similar, the volume occupied by each atom in the metal structure is nearly the same for both elements. This means volume is not the primary reason for the difference in density.

steps.title: Analyze the mass factor
steps.content:
Copper ($Z=29$) is further to the right in the transition metal series than Iron ($Z=26$). Consequently, Copper has a significantly higher relative atomic mass ($A_r \approx 63.55$) compared to Iron ($A_r \approx 55.85$). 

This increase in mass is much larger than the very small increase in atomic radius.

steps.title: Compare the ratios
steps.content:
Since the atomic mass increases significantly while the volume remains nearly constant, the mass per unit volume must increase. 

The data confirms this: Copper has a higher density of **8.96 g cm$^{-3}$** compared to Iron's **7.87 g cm$^{-3}$**. The higher mass of Copper outweighs the slight difference in atomic radius.

gdc.instructions: Evaluate the mass increase ratio and the volume increase ratio. Mass: 63.55 / 55.85. Volume: (128 / 126)^3.

ti84.instructions: Compare the mass ratio to the volume ratio. For mass: 63.55 / 55.85. For volume: (128 / 126)^3.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the ratio of masses and compare it to the ratio of volumes ($r^3$).

calculator.description: We can verify that the percentage increase in mass is greater than the percentage increase in volume.

parts.content: Calculate the difference in first ionization energy between zinc (Zn) and scandium (Sc). Suggest why zinc has weaker metallic bonding than scandium, referencing its electron configuration.
parts.markscheme:
- Calculation of difference: $906 - 631$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Correct answer with units: $275 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$ <marks>1 mark</marks>
- Zinc has a filled d-subshell ($3d^{10}$) / d-electrons do not participate in the metallic bond, resulting in fewer delocalized electrons and weaker bonding <marks>1 mark</marks>
<note>Units must be included in final answer</note>

steps.title: Calculate the IE difference
steps.content:
To find the difference in first ionization energy between zinc and scandium, we subtract the value for scandium from the value for zinc provided in the table.

$$\text{Difference} = 906 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} - 631 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1} = 275 \text{ kJ mol}^{-1}$$

Remember to include the units $(\text{kJ mol}^{-1})$ in your final answer to secure the second mark according to the markscheme.

calculator.description: Calculate the numerical difference between the ionization energies.

steps.title: Compare electron configurations
steps.content:
Next, let's look at the electron configurations of both elements to understand their bonding capabilities. This involves concepts from **Topic S1.3: Electron configurations**.

- **Scandium (Sc):** $[\text{Ar}] 3d^1 4s^2$
- **Zinc (Zn):** $[\text{Ar}] 3d^{10} 4s^2$

Scandium has an incomplete d-subshell, while Zinc has a completely **filled d-subshell** ($3d^{10}$). This structural difference is the key to their different bonding strengths.

steps.title: Explain metallic bonding strength
steps.content:
Metallic bonding strength depends on the number of delocalized electrons contributing to the 'sea' of electrons (see **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**).

In Zinc, the **filled $3d^{10}$ subshell** is very stable and held relatively close to the nucleus. As a result, these d-electrons do not easily delocalize into the metallic lattice. Zinc effectively only delocalizes its two $4s$ electrons.

In contrast, Scandium (and other transition metals) can delocalize both its $4s$ and $3d$ electrons. Because Zinc has **fewer delocalized electrons**, the electrostatic attraction between the metal cations and the sea of electrons is weaker, leading to **weaker metallic bonding**.

parts.content: State the full electronic configuration of $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$.
parts.markscheme:
$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^3$ <marks>A1</marks>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Start with neutral Chromium
steps.content:
To find the configuration of the $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ion, we first look at a neutral Chromium atom. Chromium has an atomic number of 24, meaning it has 24 electrons. 

You might expect the configuration to follow the standard filling order ($4s^2 3d^4$), but Chromium is a well-known exception in the IB syllabus due to the stability of half-filled subshells.

steps.title: Identify the Chromium anomaly
steps.content:
The actual electron configuration for neutral $\text{Cr}$ is:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^1 3d^5$$

Instead of $4s^2 3d^4$, one electron from the $4s$ orbital is promoted to the $3d$ orbital. This creates two half-filled subshells ($4s^1$ and $3d^5$), which is a lower energy, more stable state for the atom.

steps.title: Remove electrons for ions
steps.content:
To form the $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ion, we must remove three electrons from the neutral atom. 

**Crucial Rule:** For transition metals, electrons are always removed from the **4s subshell before the 3d subshell**, even though the $3d$ subshell was filled last. This is because once the orbitals are occupied, the $4s$ electrons are further from the nucleus and higher in energy.

steps.title: Stepwise electron removal
steps.content:
Let's remove the 3 electrons from $1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 4s^1 3d^5$ one by one:

1.  **Remove 1st electron:** It comes from the $4s$ subshell $\rightarrow 4s^0$.
2.  **Remove 2nd electron:** Since $4s$ is now empty, we take it from the $3d$ subshell $\rightarrow 3d^4$.
3.  **Remove 3rd electron:** We take another from the $3d$ subshell $\rightarrow 3d^3$.

steps.title: State final configuration
steps.content:
After removing those 3 electrons, we are left with the following full electronic configuration for $\text{Cr}^{3+}$:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^3$$

Note that the $4s^0$ is usually omitted in the final answer as it is empty.

highlights.text: State the full electronic configuration of $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$.

steps.title: Identify number of electrons
steps.content:
First, we look at the periodic table to find the atomic number of Chromium ($Cr$), which is 24. This means a neutral Chromium atom has 24 protons and 24 electrons.

Since we are looking for the configuration of the $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ion, we need to account for the $3+$ charge. A positive charge indicates that electrons have been lost.

highlights.text: State the full electronic configuration of $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$

steps.title: Calculate the electron count
steps.content:
To find the total number of electrons in the $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ ion, we subtract the charge from the atomic number:

$$\text{Electrons} = 24 - 3 = 21$$

We need to distribute these 21 electrons into the subshells.

gdc.instructions: Subtract the charge (3) from the atomic number of Chromium (24).

ti84.instructions: Enter the atomic number minus the charge.

desmos.instructions: Calculate the total electrons by subtracting the charge from the atomic number.

calculator.description: Verify the number of electrons for Cr3+.

steps.title: Fill subshells in order
steps.content:
Using the Direct Subshell Filling approach for transition metal ions, we fill the orbitals in order of increasing energy. A key rule for transition metal ions is that the $4s$ subshell is higher in energy than the $3d$ subshell, so the $4s$ remains empty and we fill $3d$ immediately after $3p$.

Assign the 21 electrons following the maximum capacity of each subshell ($s=2, p=6, d=10$):

1.  **1s**: 2 electrons $\rightarrow 1s^2$ (Total: 2)
2.  **2s**: 2 electrons $\rightarrow 2s^2$ (Total: 4)
3.  **2p**: 6 electrons $\rightarrow 2p^6$ (Total: 10)
4.  **3s**: 2 electrons $\rightarrow 3s^2$ (Total: 12)
5.  **3p**: 6 electrons $\rightarrow 3p^6$ (Total: 18)

We have $21 - 18 = 3$ electrons remaining.

steps.title: Assign remaining electrons
steps.content:
The remaining 3 electrons are placed directly into the $3d$ subshell because, in transition metal ions, the $4s$ subshell is not occupied.

$$3d^3$$

This brings our total count to 21 electrons.

gdc.instructions: Add up the electrons in each subshell to verify the total.

ti84.instructions: Sum the exponents of the configuration.

calculator.description: Sum the electrons to ensure they equal 21.

steps.title: State the final configuration
steps.content:
Combining all the subshells in order, we get the full electronic configuration for $\text{Cr}^{3+}$. 

This matches the requirement for the **1 mark** available in the markscheme:

$$1s^2 2s^2 2p^6 3s^2 3p^6 3d^3$$

parts.content: Explain why chromium shows variable oxidation states, while calcium does not.
parts.markscheme:
Chromium has electrons in both $3d$ and $4s$ subshells which are close in energy, allowing it to lose varying numbers of electrons <marks>M1</marks>

Calcium only has electrons in the $4s$ subshell available for bonding and has a large increase in ionization energy after these are removed / limits it to a $+2$ oxidation state <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify electron configurations
steps.content:
To understand oxidation states, we first look at the electron configurations of both elements. These configurations show which electrons are available for bonding.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Ca: } & [Ar] 4s^2 \\ \text{Cr: } & [Ar] 3d^5 4s^1 \end{aligned}$$

This highlights the different subshells that might be involved in losing electrons.

highlights.text: chromium
calcium

steps.title: Analyze chromium's energy proximity
steps.content: In chromium, the **$4s$ and $3d$ subshells are very close in energy**. Because the energy difference (proximity) between these subshells is small, electrons from both can be lost or shared during chemical reactions. This allows chromium to achieve various stable oxidation states, such as $+2, +3,$ and $+6$.

highlights.text: variable oxidation states

steps.title: Analyze calcium's energy gap
steps.content:
For calcium, only the two electrons in the **$4s$ subshell** are valence electrons. The next available electrons are in the $3p$ subshell, which is a stable core level significantly lower in energy. 

There is a **large energy gap** between the $4s$ and $3p$ subshells. This leads to a massive increase in ionization energy after the first two electrons are removed, effectively limiting calcium to a single $+2$ oxidation state.

steps.title: Summarize the key difference
steps.content:
The key reason for the difference is the energy relationship between subshells:

*   **Chromium:** The small energy gap between $3d$ and $4s$ allows for flexibility in the number of electrons lost.
*   **Calcium:** The large energy gap between $4s$ and the core $3p$ electrons prevents the loss of more than two electrons.

This concept is a fundamental part of **Topic S3.1.9: Oxidation states of transition elements**.

steps.title: Look at electron configurations
steps.content:
To understand ionization energies, we first look at the electron configurations of both elements:

*   **Calcium ($Z=20$):** $[Ar] 4s^2$
*   **Chromium ($Z=24$):** $[Ar] 3d^5 4s^1$

This shows us which electrons are in the outer subshells and available for removal during ionization.

steps.title: Analyze Calcium's ionization energy
steps.content:
Calcium has two electrons in its $4s$ subshell. Removing these two electrons forms the stable $\text{Ca}^{2+}$ ion, which has a noble gas configuration ($[Ar]$).

If we try to remove a third electron, it must come from the stable $3p$ subshell. This requires a massive amount of energy because the electron is closer to the nucleus and less shielded. In a graph of successive ionization energies, we see a **large jump** after the second electron is removed, which limits calcium to the $+2$ oxidation state.

highlights.text: large increase in ionization energy after these are removed / limits it to a +2 oxidation state

steps.title: Analyze Chromium's ionization energy
steps.content:
Chromium is a transition metal. Its $4s$ and $3d$ subshells are very **close in energy**. 

Because these levels are so similar, the successive ionization energies for chromium increase relatively gradually as electrons are removed from both the $4s$ and the $3d$ subshells. There is no immediate massive energy jump after the first or second electron is lost. This allows chromium to lose different numbers of electrons (e.g., $+2, +3, +6$) depending on the chemical environment.

highlights.text: Chromium has electrons in both 3d and 4s subshells which are close in energy, allowing it to lose varying numbers of electrons

steps.title: Synthesize the final answer
steps.content:
In summary, the key difference lies in the energy gap:

1.  **Chromium** can show variable oxidation states because the energy difference between the $4s$ and $3d$ subshells is small, allowing for a gradual increase in successive ionization energies.
2.  **Calcium** is restricted to $+2$ because removing a third electron involves a massive jump in ionization energy as it breaks into a stable noble gas core.

parts.content: Calculate the oxidation state of chromium in $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$.
parts.markscheme:
Let oxidation state of Cr be $x$
Oxidation state of O = $-2$
$2x + 3(-2) = 0$
$2x = 6$
$x = +3$
Oxidation state of Cr = $+3$ <marks>A1</marks>

<note>Award mark for correct answer with or without working.</note>

[1 mark]

steps.title: Identify the compound type
steps.content: To find the oxidation state of chromium in $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$, we first recognize that this is a neutral compound. According to the rules of oxidation states (which you can find in **Topic S3.1.6 Oxidation states**), the sum of all oxidation states in a neutral molecule must be equal to zero.

highlights.text: $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$

steps.title: Assign known oxidation states
steps.content:
Oxygen almost always has an oxidation state of $-2$ when it is in a compound (except in peroxides or when bonded to fluorine). 

Let's define $x$ as the oxidation state of a single chromium atom.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
We have two chromium atoms and three oxygen atoms in the formula $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$. Using the algebraic summation method, we set the total sum to zero:

$$2(x) + 3(-2) = 0$$

This simplifies to:

$$2x - 6 = 0$$

steps.title: Solve for chromium
steps.content:
Now, we just need to isolate $x$ using basic algebra:

$$\begin{aligned} 2x &= +6 \\ x &= \frac{+6}{2} \\ x &= +3 \end{aligned}$$

calculator.description: Verify the division to find the oxidation state per chromium atom.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The oxidation state of chromium is **$+3$**. 

In IB Chemistry, always remember to include the sign ($+$ or $-$) before the number when writing oxidation states! This distinguishes them from ion charges (like $3+$).

highlights.text: Calculate the oxidation state of chromium

steps.title: Identify the formula subscripts
steps.content:
To use the **Ionic Deduction Method**, we start by looking at the chemical formula provided: $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$. 

In this formula, the subscripts tell us the ratio of atoms:
- There are **2** Chromium (Cr) atoms.
- There are **3** Oxygen (O) atoms.

This method is related to the **Ionic Model** covered in **Topic S2.1**, where we learn how binary compounds are formed from ions.

highlights.text: $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$

steps.title: Reverse the cross-over rule
steps.content:
When forming an ionic compound, we often use the 'cross-over rule' where the charge of one ion becomes the subscript of the other. To find the oxidation state, we simply reverse this process:

1. Move the subscript of Oxygen (**3**) up to become the charge of Chromium.
2. Move the subscript of Chromium (**2**) up to become the charge of Oxygen.

$$\text{Cr}_2 \text{O}_3 \rightarrow \text{Cr}^{3+} \text{ and } \text{O}^{2-}$$

steps.title: Determine the oxidation state
steps.content:
From our reverse cross-over, we see that the Chromium ion has a charge of $+3$. 

In a simple ionic model, the oxidation state is equal to the charge of the ion. Since Chromium is a metal and Oxygen is a non-metal, the Chromium will have the positive value.

- Charge on $\text{Cr}$ = $+3$
- Charge on $\text{O}$ = $-2$

This is consistent with Oxygen's standard oxidation state of $-2$.

steps.title: Verify the total charge
steps.content:
We can quickly verify that the sum of oxidation states in the neutral compound $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$ equals zero:

$$\begin{aligned} 2(\text{Cr}) + 3(\text{O}) &= 0 \\ 2(+3) + 3(-2) &= 6 - 6 \\ &= 0 \end{aligned}$$

The oxidation state of chromium is **+3**.

parts.content: Describe the bonding in chromium metal, $\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{s})$, and compare it to the bonding in $\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$.
parts.markscheme:
In chromium metal, bonding is metallic, involving a lattice of positive ions/cations surrounded by a "sea" of delocalized electrons <marks>A1</marks>

In $\mathrm{Cr}_2\mathrm{O}_3$, bonding is ionic, involving the electrostatic attraction between $\mathrm{Cr}^{3+}$ ions and $\mathrm{O}^{2-}$ ions <marks>A1</marks>

[2 marks]

steps.title: Identify substance types
steps.content:
To compare the bonding, we first identify the type of substance for each. 

*   **Chromium ($\text{Cr}$)** is a transition metal, so it will exhibit **metallic bonding**.
*   **Chromium(III) oxide ($\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$)** is a compound formed between a metal (chromium) and a non-metal (oxygen), which typically results in **ionic bonding**.

You can review these models in **Topic S2: Models of bonding and structure**.

highlights.text: chromium metal, $\mathrm{Cr}(\mathrm{s})$
$\mathrm{Cr}_2 \mathrm{O}_3$

steps.title: Describe metallic bonding
steps.content:
In solid chromium metal, the atoms lose their outer electrons to form a regular lattice of **positive ions (cations)**. These electrons are no longer associated with a single nucleus and are free to move throughout the structure. 

The bonding is the **electrostatic attraction** between this lattice of positive ions and the **"sea" of delocalized electrons**.

steps.title: Describe ionic bonding
steps.content:
In chromium(III) oxide, electrons are transferred from chromium to oxygen. This creates $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ ions. 

The bonding here is **ionic**, which consists of the **electrostatic attraction** between these **oppositely charged ions** arranged in a giant 3D lattice.

steps.title: Summary of comparison
steps.content:
To get full marks, ensure you mention the specific particles and the forces involved for both:

1.  **$\text{Cr}$:** Metallic bonding; attraction between a lattice of $\text{Cr}$ cations and delocalized electrons.
2.  **$\text{Cr}_2\text{O}_3$:** Ionic bonding; attraction between $\text{Cr}^{3+}$ and $\text{O}^{2-}$ ions.

parts.content: State two factors that influence the electrical conductivity of a metal.
parts.markscheme:
- State the number of delocalized (free) electrons per atom or unit volume. <marks>A1</marks>
- State the mobility of electrons, which is determined by the lattice structure and atomic size. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Understand the Metallic Model
steps.content: To answer this, we look at **Structure 2.3: The metallic model**. In this model, a metal is described as a lattice of positive metal ions (cations) surrounded by a "sea" of delocalized valence electrons. Electrical conductivity occurs because these electrons are free to move throughout the structure when a voltage is applied.

steps.title: Identify Factor 1: Electron Density
steps.content:
The first factor is the **number of delocalized (free) electrons**. Since electricity is the flow of charge, having more charge carriers available increases the potential for conductivity. 

This is usually determined by the number of valence electrons each atom contributes to the "sea." For example, Aluminum ($3$ valence electrons) typically has more delocalized electrons per atom than Sodium ($1$ valence electron).

steps.title: Identify Factor 2: Electron Mobility
steps.content:
The second factor is the **mobility of the electrons**. Even if there are many electrons, they must be able to move easily through the lattice. 

This mobility is influenced by the **lattice structure** and **atomic size**. If the ions in the lattice are packed in a way that creates more resistance or if the atomic radius is larger, it can impact how freely the "sea" of electrons flows under an electric field.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
According to the markscheme, the two factors you should state are:

1. The **number of delocalized electrons** per atom or unit volume.
2. The **mobility of electrons**, which is determined by the lattice structure or atomic size.

steps.title: Analyze charge flow physics
steps.content:
To understand what influences electrical conductivity ($\sigma$), we look at **Electrical Transport Theory**. This theory describes how charge moves through a material based on the equation:

$$\sigma = n \cdot q \cdot \mu$$

Where:
- $n$ is the density of charge carriers.
- $q$ is the charge of each carrier (for metals, this is the electron charge).
- $\mu$ is the drift mobility of those carriers.

From this relationship, we can identify the two main physical factors that determine how well a metal conducts.

steps.title: Factor 1: Charge carrier density
steps.content:
The first factor is the concentration of available charge carriers ($n$). In metals, these are the **delocalized (free) electrons**. 

The more delocalized electrons a metal has per unit volume (or per atom), the higher the potential for current to flow. For example, Aluminum has more delocalized electrons per atom than Sodium, contributing to differences in their conductivity.

highlights.text: State the number of delocalized (free) electrons per atom or unit volume.

steps.title: Factor 2: Electron mobility
steps.content:
The second factor is **mobility** ($\mu$), which describes how easily electrons move through the metal lattice when an electric field is applied.

Mobility is limited by "collisions" or interference from the metal's internal structure. This is influenced by:
- The **lattice structure**: How the ions are arranged.
- **Atomic size**: Larger ions or irregularities in the lattice can impede the flow of electrons, reducing mobility.

highlights.text: State the mobility of electrons, which is determined by the lattice structure and atomic size.

steps.title: Final Summary
steps.content:
Based on the transport of charge, the two factors required for the marks are:

1.  **The number (density) of delocalized electrons**.
2.  **The mobility of the electrons** (determined by the lattice and atomic properties).

parts.content: Aluminium is commonly used in power lines, while lithium is not. Explain, using the metallic bonding model, why aluminium is a better electrical conductor than lithium.
parts.markscheme:
- State that each Aluminium atom contributes 3 delocalized electrons to the sea of electrons, whereas each lithium atom contributes only 1. <marks>A1</marks>
- Explain that the greater charge density and smaller metallic radius in $\text{Al}$ enhances the concentration and movement of delocalized electrons compared to $\text{Li}$. <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Compare delocalized electron contribution
steps.content:
According to the metallic bonding model, the number of delocalized electrons depends on the number of valence electrons an atom contributes to the "sea of electrons." 

*   **Aluminium (Group 13)**: Each atom contributes 3 valence electrons to the delocalized sea.
*   **Lithium (Group 1)**: Each atom contributes only 1 valence electron.

This higher contribution from aluminium results in a significantly higher concentration of mobile charge carriers.

highlights.text: Aluminium atom contributes 3 delocalized electrons to the sea of electrons, whereas each lithium atom contributes only 1

steps.title: Analyze charge density
steps.content:
Electrical conductivity is also influenced by how these electrons are packed and move within the lattice. Aluminium cations ($Al^{3+}$) have a higher charge and a **greater charge density** than lithium cations ($Li^{+}$). 

Additionally, the metallic radius of Al is effectively smaller in the lattice context compared to Li when considering the concentration of the electron sea. This combination of higher charge and smaller radius creates a more "dense" sea of electrons, which enhances the material's ability to conduct an electric current.

highlights.text: greater charge density and smaller metallic radius in Al

steps.title: Relate to electrical conductivity
steps.content:
Electrical conductivity is defined by the movement of charged particles. Because aluminium has triple the number of delocalized electrons per atom compared to lithium, there are more electrons available to flow through the metal when a potential difference is applied. 

Therefore, the higher electron concentration and the specific density of the $Al^{3+}$ lattice make aluminium a far superior conductor for applications like power lines.

parts.content: Suggest one industrial application of a highly conductive metal and relate it to its conductivity.
parts.markscheme:
- Suggest an application such as electrical wiring or power transmission cables, where high conductivity ensures efficient energy transfer with minimal loss. <marks>A1</marks>

1 mark total

steps.title: Identify an industrial application
steps.content:
A primary industrial application of highly conductive metals like copper or aluminum is in **electrical wiring** or **long-distance power transmission cables**.

This relates to the concept of metallic bonding covered in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**, where a "sea" of delocalized electrons allows for the efficient flow of electric charge.

highlights.text: electrical wiring or power transmission cables

steps.title: Relate conductivity to efficiency
steps.content:
High conductivity means the metal offers very low resistance to the flow of electricity. In large-scale power systems, using metals with high conductivity is crucial because it **minimizes energy loss as heat** during transmission. 

By reducing the resistance ($R$), the amount of electrical energy converted into thermal energy is significantly decreased, making the energy transfer far more efficient.

highlights.text: high conductivity ensures efficient energy transfer with minimal loss

steps.title: Final answer for marks
steps.content:
To earn the mark, you must state both the application and why conductivity matters:

**Application:** Electrical power transmission cables.
**Reason:** Highly conductive metals (like Aluminum) ensure efficient energy transfer with minimal loss to the surroundings as heat.

steps.title: Understand metallic conductivity
steps.content: To answer this, we look at **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**. Metals conduct electricity because they consist of a lattice of positive ions surrounded by a "sea" of delocalized electrons. These electrons are free to move throughout the structure when a potential difference is applied. Metals like silver and gold are among the most efficient at this process.

steps.title: Industrial application: Microelectronics
steps.content: An important industrial application for these metals is in **precision electronic contacts** and **microchip interconnects**. In these high-performance systems, even the smallest amount of resistance can cause significant issues.

highlights.text: highly conductive metal

steps.title: Relating to conductivity
steps.content: This application relies on high conductivity to ensure **high signal integrity**. Because silver and gold allow for extremely reliable electrical flow with minimal resistance, they prevent energy loss and signal degradation in small-scale components, which is essential for the speed and reliability of modern computers and sensors.
