parts.content: Describe and explain the trend in melting points observed in the table. How does the number of valence electrons impact the melting points?
parts.markscheme:
- Melting points generally increase with number of valence electrons <marks>A1</marks>

- More valence electrons create stronger metallic bonds due to:
  - Greater electron delocalization/"sea" of electrons
  - Increased attraction between cations and delocalized electrons <marks>A1</marks>

- Exceptions exist (e.g. Cu, Fe) due to other factors:
  - Atomic size differences
  - Different crystal/lattice structures <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Award full marks only if both the trend AND explanation with examples are provided</note>

steps.title: Identify the primary trend
steps.content:
By looking at the main group elements in the table (**Li**, **Mg**, and **Al**), we can see a clear pattern: as the number of valence electrons increases from 1 to 3, the melting point also increases.

- **Li** (1 valence electron): $180^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Mg** (2 valence electrons): $650^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **Al** (3 valence electrons): $660^{\circ}\text{C}$

This trend is a core concept in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**.

highlights.text: Melting points generally increase with number of valence electrons

steps.title: Explain metallic bond strength
steps.content:
Metallic bonding is defined as the electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions (cations) and a "sea" of delocalized valence electrons. 

When a metal has more valence electrons, it contributes more electrons to this delocalized sea. This increases the overall density of the "electron cloud" surrounding the ions, which leads to a stronger attraction throughout the lattice.

highlights.text: Greater electron delocalization/"sea" of electrons

steps.title: Analyze cation charge density
steps.content:
As we move from **Li** to **Al**, the charge on the metal cation increases:

$$\text{Li}^+ < \text{Mg}^{2+} < \text{Al}^{3+}$$

A higher charge on the cation results in a much stronger electrostatic attraction to the delocalized electrons. Because these bonds are harder to break, more thermal energy is required to melt the metal, resulting in the higher melting points observed in the data.

highlights.text: Increased attraction between cations and delocalized electrons

steps.title: Address experimental exceptions
steps.content:
While the valence electron count is a primary driver, the data for **Cu** ($1085^{\circ}\text{C}$) and **Fe** ($1538^{\circ}\text{C}$) shows that other factors are at play. These transition metals have much higher melting points due to:
1. **Atomic size**: Smaller radii allow for closer packing and stronger attractions.
2. **Lattice structure**: Different arrangements of atoms in space (crystal structures) can be more stable than others.
3. **d-electrons**: (For HL students) Inner d-electrons can also contribute to the bonding strength.

highlights.text: Exceptions exist (e.g. Cu, Fe) due to other factors

steps.title: Identify the General Trend
steps.content:
Looking at the first three elements in the table—Lithium, Magnesium, and Aluminum—we can see a clear pattern. As we move from Li to Al, the number of valence electrons increases from $1$ to $3$, and the melting point follows this upward trajectory, rising from $180^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $660^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**General Trend:** The melting point generally increases as the number of valence electrons increases.

steps.title: Explain Metallic Bond Strength
steps.content:
To understand why this trend exists, we look at the **metallic model** (Topic S2.3). Metallic bonding is the electrostatic attraction between a lattice of positive metal ions and a 'sea' of delocalized electrons.

When a metal has more valence electrons:
1. There is a **greater density** of delocalized electrons.
2. The metal cations usually have a **higher charge** (e.g., $Al^{3+}$ vs $Li^{+}$).

These two factors combined result in a stronger attraction between the cations and the electron sea, requiring more thermal energy to overcome the bonds and melt the metal.

steps.title: Contrast with Transition Metals
steps.content:
If valence electrons were the *only* factor, Copper (1 valence electron) should have a similar melting point to Lithium, and Iron (2 valence electrons) should be similar to Magnesium. However, the data shows a massive discrepancy:

- **Copper ($1085^{\circ}\text{C}$)** is over 6 times higher than **Lithium ($180^{\circ}\text{C}$)**.
- **Iron ($1538^{\circ}\text{C}$)** is more than double **Magnesium ($650^{\circ}\text{C}$)**.

This highlights that while the 'valence electron rule' works well for main-group metals, it is an incomplete picture for transition metals.

gdc.instructions: Perform 1085 / 180 and 1538 / 650 to find the scale of the anomaly.

ti84.instructions: Divide the melting point of Copper by Lithium, then Iron by Magnesium.

desmos.instructions: Compare the ratios of transition metals to s-block metals.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of melting points between transition metals and main-group metals with the same valence count.

steps.title: Analyze the Anomalies
steps.content:
The exceptionally high melting points of Copper and Iron are due to factors beyond just the number of 'outer' valence electrons:

1. **Atomic Radius:** Transition metals often have smaller atomic radii than Group 1 or 2 metals in the same period. A smaller radius allows the delocalized electrons to be closer to the nucleus, strengthening the bond.
2. **Involvement of d-electrons:** In transition metals, 3d electrons can also become partially delocalized, effectively increasing the 'bonding density'.
3. **Lattice Structure:** The way atoms are packed (their crystal structure) impacts how efficiently the metallic bonds hold the lattice together.

steps.title: Final Synthesis for Marks
steps.content:
To secure all 3 marks, your answer should summarize these points:

- **Mark 1 (Trend):** State that melting points generally increase with the number of valence electrons (seen in Li, Mg, Al).
- **Mark 2 (Mechanism):** Explain that more valence electrons increase the density of the delocalized 'sea', strengthening the attraction to the cations.
- **Mark 3 (Anomalies):** Explicitly mention that Cu and Fe are exceptions, showing much higher melting points due to their smaller atomic radii and more complex d-electron interactions/lattice structures.

highlights.text: Melting points generally increase with number of valence electrons

parts.content: Based on the data, explain why aluminum Al has higher electrical conductivity than magnesium Mg.
parts.markscheme:
- Aluminum has three valence electrons (compared to magnesium's two) <marks>A1</marks>

- More free electrons in the electron "sea" leads to greater ease of electron flow/higher conductivity <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

steps.title: Compare valence electrons
steps.content:
To explain the difference in conductivity, we first look at the number of valence electrons for each metal provided in the table. 

*   **Magnesium (Mg)** has 2 valence electrons.
*   **Aluminum (Al)** has 3 valence electrons.

In metallic bonding (which you can review in **Topic S2.3.1**), these valence electrons are lost from individual atoms to form a "sea" of delocalized electrons that surround the positive metal ions.

highlights.text: Number of Valence Electrons
Mg
Al

steps.title: Analyze electron density
steps.content:
Electrical conductivity is the ability of a material to allow the flow of electric charge. In metals, this charge is carried by the delocalized electrons. 

Because an aluminum atom provides **three** electrons to the delocalized sea while a magnesium atom only provides **two**, aluminum has a higher density of free-moving charge carriers per unit volume.

steps.title: Relate to conductivity
steps.content:
With a greater number of delocalized electrons available to move through the lattice, there is a greater ease of electron flow when a potential difference is applied. This directly results in the higher experimental electrical conductivity value for Al ($3.7 \times 10^5$ S/cm) compared to Mg ($2.3 \times 10^5$ S/cm).

**Marking points:**
*   **1 mark** for identifying that Al has more valence electrons than Mg.
*   **1 mark** for explaining that more delocalized electrons lead to greater charge transport/conductivity.

parts.content: Calculate the ratio of the electrical conductivity of copper Cu to lithium Li.
parts.markscheme:
- Write ratio formula: $\text{Conductivity ratio} = \frac{\text{Conductivity of Cu}}{\text{Conductivity of Li}}$ <marks>M1</marks>

- Substitute values and calculate: $\frac{5.8 \times 10^5}{1.1 \times 10^5} = 5.27$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award full marks for correct final answer with working shown</note>

steps.title: Identify values from table
steps.content:
To calculate the ratio, we first need to extract the electrical conductivity values for Copper ($Cu$) and Lithium ($Li$) from the experimental data table provided in the question.

- Electrical conductivity of $Li = 1.1 \times 10^5 \text{ S/cm}$
- Electrical conductivity of $Cu = 5.8 \times 10^5 \text{ S/cm}$

steps.title: State the ratio formula
steps.content:
The question asks for the ratio of Copper to Lithium. We set this up as a fraction:

$$\text{Conductivity ratio} = \frac{\text{Conductivity of Cu}}{\text{Conductivity of Li}}$$

Substituting the values from the table:

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{5.8 \times 10^5}{1.1 \times 10^5}$$

Establishing this formula correctly is often worth the first mark (**M1**) in IB chemistry problems.

steps.title: Calculate the numerical ratio
steps.content:
Notice that the $10^5$ terms in the numerator and denominator cancel out, simplifying the calculation to $5.8 \div 1.1$.

$$\text{Ratio} = \frac{5.8}{1.1} \approx 5.27$$

Providing the final numerical answer earns the second mark (**A1**).

desmos.instructions: Type the fraction 5.8/1.1 to get the result.

calculator.description: Calculate the ratio of the two conductivity values.

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content: The ratio of the electrical conductivity of copper to lithium is **5.27**.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: S2.3.1 Metallic bonding and properties of metals
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: All parts focus on the metallic model: defining metallic bonding, describing the lattice of positive ions with delocalized electrons, and linking this structure to malleability (a key property of metals).

parts.content: Define the term metallic bonding.
parts.markscheme:
Electrostatic attraction between positive metal ions and delocalized electrons 
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the chemical species
steps.content: To define metallic bonding, we first look at the components of a metal lattice. Metals consist of a regular arrangement of positive metal ions (cations). These cations are formed when metal atoms lose their outer-shell electrons. This concept is explored in **Structure 2.3: The metallic model** of your syllabus.

steps.title: Describe the electron behavior
steps.content: The valence electrons lost by the metal atoms are not bound to any single nucleus. Instead, they are **delocalized**, meaning they are free to move throughout the entire structure. This is often described as a "sea of delocalized electrons."

steps.title: Define the bonding force
steps.content: Bonding is always about the attraction between different particles. In this case, there is a strong **electrostatic attraction** between the positively charged metal ions and the negatively charged delocalized electrons. This attraction is what holds the metal structure together.

steps.title: State the formal definition
steps.content:
Combining these points, the formal definition required for the mark is:

**Metallic bonding is the electrostatic attraction between positive metal ions and delocalized electrons.**

highlights.text: Define the term metallic bonding

parts.content: Describe the arrangement of particles in a metallic structure.
parts.markscheme:
- Lattice of positive metal ions 
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Surrounded by a “sea” of delocalized electrons 
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Identify the key components
steps.content: To describe the metallic model, we need to identify the two main types of "particles" involved and how they are organized. According to the IB syllabus in **Topic S2.3: The metallic model**, metals are described as a collection of positive ions within a mobile cloud of electrons.

highlights.text: arrangement of particles

steps.title: Describe the cation lattice
steps.content:
In a metallic structure, metal atoms lose their outer (valence) electrons to become **positive metal ions** (also called cations). These ions are not floating randomly; they are arranged in a fixed, regular, repeating three-dimensional pattern known as a **lattice**.

This is the first essential point for your marks.

steps.title: Describe the delocalized electrons
steps.content:
The valence electrons that were lost by the atoms do not belong to any single nucleus. Instead, they are **delocalized**, meaning they are free to move throughout the entire structure. We describe this arrangement as a **"sea" of delocalized electrons** that surrounds the positive ions.

This "sea" is what allows metals to conduct electricity so well!

steps.title: Summary of the arrangement
steps.content:
To get full marks on this question, your description should combine these two components:

1. A **lattice of positive metal ions** (cations).
2. A **"sea" of delocalized electrons** surrounding them.

The electrostatic attraction between these two components is what we call a metallic bond.

parts.content: Explain why metals are malleable.
parts.markscheme:
- Layers of metal ions can slide over each other 
<marks>1 mark</marks>
- Metallic bonding remains due to mobile electrons 
<marks>1 mark</marks>

steps.title: Visualize the metallic lattice
steps.content:
To understand malleability, we first need to look at the internal structure of a metal. Metals consist of a regular lattice of positive metal ions (cations) surrounded by a "sea" of delocalized valence electrons. This model is a core part of **Syllabus Topic S2.3.1: Metallic bonding and properties of metals**.

Because these electrons are not fixed to any single atom, the bonding is described as **non-directional**.

steps.title: Explain the sliding layers
steps.content: In a solid metal, the cations are arranged in regular, closely packed layers. When an external force is applied (like hitting the metal with a hammer), these layers of metal ions can slide over each other into new positions without the structure shattering.

highlights.text: Layers of metal ions can slide over each other

steps.title: Role of delocalized electrons
steps.content:
The reason the metal doesn't simply fall apart when the layers move is due to the delocalized electrons. Because these electrons are mobile, they act like a "glue" that adjusts to the new positions of the cations. 

The electrostatic attraction between the cations and the sea of electrons is maintained regardless of the shift, allowing the metallic bond to remain intact.

highlights.text: Metallic bonding remains due to mobile electrons

steps.title: Summary of marks
steps.content:
In an IB exam, you earn the two marks by identifying these two structural features:
1. The ability of **layers of ions to slide** over each other ($1$ mark).
2. The fact that **metallic bonding is maintained** by the movement of **delocalized/mobile electrons** ($1$ mark).
